title: Replace last-write-wins doc sync with Yjs CRDT to stop lost edits in concurrent rich-text editing
business_context: A B2B docs product (like a Notion competitor) lets teams co-edit pages. Support tickets show ~40 'my paragraph disappeared' reports/week. The current sync saves the whole document body on every keystroke debounce and the last save wins, silently clobbering a colleague's concurrent edits. 12k daily active editors, average 3.2 collaborators per active doc.
constraints: Must preserve full edit history for legal hold (7-year retention) — cannot drop ops
Backward-compat: 2M existing documents stored as plain HTML strings must keep opening
p95 keystroke-to-remote-render < 120ms across 3 collaborators in same region
Server is horizontally scaled (6 Node instances behind ALB) — no sticky sessions guaranteed
Offline edits on mobile must merge without manual conflict resolution on reconnect

before_state.description: Whole-document last-write-wins over a debounced REST PUT. Concurrent editors overwrite each other; no per-character merge.

issues: Last-write-wins overwrites concurrent edits with no merge
Sends full HTML body each save — O(doc size) network per keystroke
No edit-level history; legal hold cannot reconstruct who changed what
500ms debounce window is a guaranteed clobber zone for 2+ editors
No remote update channel — peers never see each other live
contentEditable innerHTML diffing loses caret and selection state

reasoning: Naive fix: add a version column and reject stale PUTs (optimistic concurrency). Rejected because it turns every concurrent edit into a 409 the user must manually reconcile — unacceptable for live co-editing where collisions are the norm, not the exception. Alternative considered: server-side OT (ShareDB) — powerful but requires a single authoritative transform server, which conflicts with our 6-instance no-sticky-session constraint and adds a SPOF. Chosen: Yjs CRDTs, which merge commutatively so any instance can apply any update in any order. Cross-layer trace: UI binds a Y.XmlFragment to ProseMirror via y-prosemirror (caret-stable); client encodes incremental Yjs updates (not full HTML) over a WebSocket; a stateless relay broadcasts opaque update binaries and appends them to an append-only updates table (legal hold); a periodic compaction job folds updates into a Y.Doc snapshot in Postgres; existing HTML docs are lazily converted to a Y.Doc on first open behind a doc_format flag; config adds Redis pub/sub so any of the 6 instances fans out to peers; tests assert convergence (two divergent client states reach identical Y.Doc) and that the updates table is append-only.

after_state.description: Yjs CRDT document bound to ProseMirror. Incremental binary updates over WebSocket, fanned out via Redis across instances, persisted append-only with periodic snapshot compaction. Legacy HTML lazily migrated.

files.purpose: Stateless WebSocket relay: persists every Yjs update append-only for legal hold and fans out across all 6 instances via Redis pub/sub (no sticky sessions needed).
Bounds replay cost by folding update logs into snapshots while keeping the raw append-only log intact for 7-year retention.
Backward-compat: converts 2M legacy HTML docs to a Y.Doc on first open, idempotently and under a row lock to avoid double-import races.
Binds Yjs to ProseMirror so edits merge per-character with stable caret/selection, plus live remote cursors via awareness.

migration_strategy: Ship Yjs tables (doc_updates, doc_snapshots) and the relay behind a per-doc collab_v2 flag defaulting off; HTML path untouched.
Enable collab_v2 for internal/dogfood workspaces; dual-read: open via ensureYDoc, keep writing legacy HTML mirror for rollback.
Canary 5% of docs; monitor convergence-error metric and p95 update-apply latency; legacy HTML mirror still written.
Ramp 5% -> 50% -> 100% over two weeks; auto-rollback a doc to HTML if convergence errors > 0 for that doc.
Stop writing the legacy HTML mirror once 100% stable for 7 days; HTML remains in cold storage for retention.
Remove the legacy PUT route and the flag after one full retention audit cycle confirms snapshots reconstruct history.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a Y.Doc seeded with a 4-paragraph article. Convergence test: spawn two in-memory clients, apply divergent concurrent ops (client A deletes para 2 while client B types into para 2), exchange updates both directions, assert encodeStateAsUpdate(A) === encodeStateAsUpdate(B) byte-for-byte and final text contains B's insertion (no lost edit). Side-effect asserts: doc_updates rows equal the number of update messages and are strictly append-only (no UPDATE except compacted flag), and after compact() the snapshot replays to identical text. Negative/chaos test: drop the Redis connection mid-session, queue 30 client ops, restore Redis, and assert all peers reconverge and no doc_updates row was lost. Legacy test: import a known HTML doc and assert the Y.Doc text matches the stripped HTML.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the 500ms debounce boundary, two editors typing within the same window each PUT the full HTML; the second PUT overwrites the first with a body that never contained the first editor's characters — the merge never happens because the unit of write is the whole document, not the edit.

title: Fix presence ghosts and stuck cursors in a collaborative whiteboard by moving from naive heartbeat to awareness with TTL and tab-visibility
business_context: A design-collaboration whiteboard (Figma-style) shows live cursors and an avatar stack of who's present. Users report 'ghost' collaborators that never leave and cursors frozen mid-canvas after someone closes a laptop lid. On boards with 30+ concurrent users this clutters the avatar bar and misleads people into thinking colleagues are active. Peak board has 80 simultaneous editors.
constraints: Presence must clear within 5s of an ungraceful disconnect (lid close, network drop)
Cursor broadcast must not exceed 30 msgs/sec/client to protect the WS fanout
Avatar stack must dedupe a single user open in multiple tabs into one entry
Works across 4 WS gateway instances with Redis fanout; no sticky sessions
Reconnect within 10s must restore the same presence identity, not create a new ghost

before_state.description: Presence kept in an in-memory map keyed by socket id; cleared only on the socket 'close' event. Lid-close/network-drop never fires close promptly, so entries leak. Cursor positions broadcast unthrottled.

issues: Relies solely on 'close' which never fires on lid-close/network-drop -> ghosts
Per-socket key means multi-tab users appear multiple times
Unthrottled cursor broadcast floods fanout at 80 users
In-memory only: instance restart drops all presence, no cross-instance view

reasoning: Naive fix: add a ping/pong and delete on missed pong. Better, but still per-socket (multi-tab dupes) and the in-memory map is invisible to the other 3 gateways, so a user on instance B is a ghost to instance A. Rejected building a custom presence protocol on raw sockets: it re-implements exactly what Yjs awareness already solves (clock-based state with automatic timeout) and we'd own the edge cases forever. Chosen: Yjs awareness for cursor/presence state with a meta clock + TTL, deduped by userId at the avatar layer, with a Redis-backed presence set so all gateways share one view. Cross-layer trace: UI throttles pointermove via rAF and writes cursor into awareness; on document.visibilitychange=hidden it clears local awareness state immediately; the gateway relays awareness updates and also mirrors a userId->lastSeen sorted set into Redis with a 5s scan that evicts and emits leave; the avatar component groups awareness states by userId so two tabs collapse to one; config: a single Redis ZSET per board scanned by one elected sweeper; tests assert eviction timing and dedupe.

after_state.description: Yjs awareness drives cursors with built-in clock TTL; visibilitychange clears presence on lid-close; Redis ZSET gives all gateways a shared presence view evicted by a 5s sweep; avatar stack dedupes by userId.

files.purpose: Caps cursor updates to one per animation frame and proactively clears presence on tab-hide/pagehide so peers stop seeing a frozen cursor.
Mirrors awareness into a Redis ZSET keyed by userId so every gateway shares one presence view, and evicts entries older than 5s (covers ungraceful drops).
Collapses multiple tabs/sockets of the same user into one avatar by grouping awareness states by userId.

migration_strategy: Deploy awareness relay alongside the legacy presence map; emit both old 'leave' and new awareness events behind presence_v2 flag off.
Turn on the Redis ZSET mirror in shadow mode (write, don't read) to validate eviction timing against real disconnect patterns.
Flip a 10% cohort to read presence from awareness+ZSET; compare ghost-count metric vs control.
Ramp to 100%, keep the legacy map running disabled for one week as instant rollback.
Delete the per-socket presence map and legacy leave broadcast once ghost-count holds at zero.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a board Awareness with 3 simulated clients, two sharing one userId (multi-tab). Assert AvatarStack renders exactly 2 avatars (dedupe). Eviction test with fake timers: client C stops updating, advance clock 5.1s, run sweep(), assert C removed from awareness states and ZSET, and a 'leave' fanned out. Side-effect assert: Redis ZSET cardinality matches distinct active userIds. Throttle test: dispatch 200 pointermove events in one frame, assert exactly one awareness cursor write occurred. Chaos test: simulate ungraceful drop (no close event) plus a Redis blip during the sweep window; assert presence still clears within 5s after Redis recovers and no live user is wrongly evicted.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the lid-close boundary the OS suspends the tab without firing a WebSocket 'close', so the only cleanup trigger never runs and the presence entry — plus its last cursor position — lives forever in the in-memory map and on every peer's canvas.

title: Make offline-first mobile note merging deterministic by replacing JSON deep-merge with Automerge change blocks
business_context: A field-service mobile app lets technicians edit job notes offline in basements and elevators, then sync when signal returns. The current sync does a recursive JSON deep-merge on reconnect; when a tech edits a checklist offline and dispatch edits the same job online, items get duplicated or silently dropped. ~6% of synced jobs show data anomalies, triggering billing disputes. 9k technicians, average 40 min offline per shift.
constraints: Must converge identically regardless of sync order (tech-first or dispatch-first)
Document includes an array of checklist items where order and per-item edits both matter
Sync payload over cellular must be incremental, not the whole document
Conflicting scalar edits (e.g., status field) must resolve deterministically and be auditable
Existing 1.4M jobs stored as plain JSON must continue to load

before_state.description: Reconnect does lodash merge of local and server JSON. Arrays merge by index, scalars by 'whoever synced last', producing duplicates and lost edits.

issues: Array merge-by-index duplicates items when both sides inserted at different positions
Scalar 'source wins' silently discards the other side with no audit trail
No notion of causality — a delete on one side reappears from the other
Pushes the entire merged document — heavy over cellular
Last syncer to PUT overwrites a third concurrent editor
No conflict surfaced to user or audit log

reasoning: Naive fix: tag each field with a timestamp and do last-writer-wins per field. Rejected because clocks on offline phones drift and an elevator-trapped tech's older-but-intended edit loses to a stale server write; also LWW still can't model array insert/delete causally. Considered server-authoritative OT, rejected because the offline window (40 min) makes server-mediated transforms impossible — the device must merge locally with no server. Chosen: Automerge, a state-based CRDT whose changes carry causal history and merge order-independently, with first-class list (array) CRDT semantics for the checklist. Cross-layer trace: UI mutates an Automerge doc via Automerge.change; sync stores only binary change blocks; on reconnect the client exchanges Automerge.getChanges deltas with the server (incremental), Automerge.merge converges; conflicting scalars are kept in Automerge conflicts() and surfaced to an audit log; legacy JSON loads via Automerge.from on first edit; tests assert order-independent convergence and conflict capture.

after_state.description: Automerge document with list CRDT for checklist. Incremental binary change exchange on reconnect, order-independent convergence, conflicts captured for audit. Legacy JSON imported lazily.

files.purpose: Wraps all mutations in Automerge.change so each carries causal metadata; list ops use the list CRDT, eliminating index-merge duplication.
Exchanges only the change blocks each side is missing (delta sync) instead of whole documents, cheap over cellular and safe with a third concurrent editor.
Server stores change blocks, serves missing deltas, applies pushes order-independently, and records scalar conflicts to an audit table.

migration_strategy: Add job_doc (change blocks) and sync_conflicts tables; keep legacy JSON column as source of truth, write-through Automerge changes behind crdt_sync flag off.
On first edit of a job, run fromLegacyJson and store the initial change; dual-write legacy JSON for rollback.
Enable crdt_sync for one region's technicians (canary); compare data-anomaly rate vs control regions.
Ramp region by region; auto-disable for a job if applyChanges throws (fallback to legacy merge) and alert.
Once anomaly rate < 0.1% for 2 weeks, stop dual-writing legacy JSON and make change blocks authoritative.
Backfill remaining 1.4M jobs lazily; only fully-migrated jobs drop the JSON column after audit.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a job with a 5-item checklist and status='enroute'. Order-independence test: tech toggles item 3 and adds item 6 offline; dispatch sets status='onsite' online; apply in both orders (tech-then-dispatch, dispatch-then-tech) and assert identical final doc with all 6 items, item 3 toggled, status='onsite', zero duplicates. Side-effect asserts: job_doc.changes count equals total changes applied; a sync_conflicts row is written exactly when both sides set status. Negative/chaos test: a malformed/truncated change block from a flaky cellular push must be rejected without corrupting stored doc (applyChanges throws -> transaction rolls back, legacy fallback kicks in). Legacy test: import a real JSON job and assert Automerge doc deep-equals the source.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the array boundary, lodash merges checklist items by array index: when the tech inserted an item at position 2 offline and dispatch inserted a different item at position 2 online, index-merge overwrites one with the other (lost edit) or shifts both into one slot (duplicate) — there is no causal identity for list elements.

title: Eliminate kanban card double-move on concurrent drag by modeling column membership as a Yjs Y.Array of fractional indices
business_context: A B2B project-management SaaS has a real-time kanban board. When two PMs drag the same card to different columns at the same time, the card ends up duplicated (appears in both columns) or vanishes. Roughly 220 boards/day hit this, generating 'where did my ticket go' tickets. Largest customer runs boards with 1,800 cards across 12 columns.
constraints: A card must exist in exactly one column after any concurrent move
Reordering within a column must not require renumbering all cards (O(1) move)
Board state syncs across web + an Electron desktop client over the same WS
Must support 50 concurrent editors per board without server-side locking
Existing boards stored as Postgres rows (card.column_id, card.position int) must migrate

before_state.description: Each move is a REST PATCH setting column_id and an integer position; server applies last-write. Concurrent moves race and the client optimistically inserts into the target column without removing from the source under conflict.

issues: Two concurrent moves both shift positions, corrupting ordering
No atomic 'remove from source + add to target' — card can land in two columns
O(n) position renumber on every move
Optimistic insert with no removal of stale copy on conflicting remote move
No live broadcast of the move to other editors
Integer index collides when two cards target the same slot

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap the move in a Postgres transaction with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the card row. It removes duplication but serializes all moves on a hot board (50 editors) and still renumbers O(n) positions, and the desktop client's optimistic UI can still show a transient duplicate. Rejected a server-authoritative lock service (Redlock) — adds a distributed-lock SPOF for a problem CRDTs solve lock-free. Chosen: model the board as a Y.Map of columns, each column a Y.Array of card ids, and order cards with fractional indexing so a move is a single atomic Y transaction (delete from source array, insert into target). Cross-layer trace: UI computes a fractional key between neighbors; a Y.Doc transaction moves the id atomically (CRDT guarantees single membership); updates broadcast over WS + Redis; a debounced projector materializes the Y.Doc back into the cards table for reporting/back-compat; legacy int positions convert to fractional keys at import; tests assert single-membership under concurrent moves.

after_state.description: Board is a Y.Doc: Y.Map<columnId, Y.Array<cardId>> with fractional-index ordering. Moves are atomic Y transactions guaranteeing exactly-one-column membership. A projector syncs to Postgres for reporting.

files.purpose: A single Y transaction removes the card from its source column array and inserts into the target with a fractional key, so the CRDT guarantees exactly-one-column membership even under concurrent moves.
Keeps the relational cards table eventually consistent with the CRDT for reporting and legacy reads, without being on the move hot path.
Converts legacy integer positions into collision-free fractional keys preserving existing order.

migration_strategy: Add frac_key column (nullable) and the Y.Doc board store behind board_crdt flag off; legacy PATCH path still active.
Run intToFractional for a board on first open under the flag; keep writing integer positions in parallel for rollback.
Canary 3% of boards to the CRDT move path; watch a single-membership invariant metric (cards in >1 column == 0).
Ramp to 100% over 10 days; if invariant ever violates for a board, freeze it back to the integer path and alert.
Stop maintaining integer positions after 2 weeks clean; projector keeps frac_key authoritative.
Drop the legacy move endpoint and position column post-audit.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 3-column board with 6 cards and known fractional keys. Concurrency test: two Y.Docs (peers) each move the same card to different columns concurrently, exchange updates, assert the card appears in exactly one column on both peers and both converge to the same column. Reorder test: 100 random moves applied in two different orders converge identically. Side-effect assert: after project(), the cards table shows each card with one column_id and the frac_keys are strictly increasing within a column. Negative/chaos test: drop a WS update mid-move then replay out of order; assert no duplicate membership and projector output unchanged. Migration test: a board with integer positions migrates to frac_keys preserving order exactly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the concurrent-move boundary, two PATCHes each run 'remove implicitly via column_id update' non-atomically across the source and target; the optimistic client that inserted into the target never receives a removal for the source copy, so the card is rendered in both columns and the integer position shifts corrupt neighbor ordering.

title: Stop spreadsheet formula corruption under concurrent cell edits by switching whole-sheet broadcast to per-cell OT with version vectors
business_context: A fintech analytics tool offers a collaborative spreadsheet for building financial models. When two analysts edit cells in the same sheet simultaneously, formulas referencing moved cells corrupt (=A1 becomes #REF or points to the wrong cell) and recalculation cascades produce wrong numbers shown to clients. A wrong NPV was shown to an investor last quarter. Sheets reach 50k populated cells; up to 8 concurrent editors.
constraints: Concurrent edits to different cells must never block each other
Formula references must stay valid when rows/columns are inserted concurrently
Recalculation must be deterministic given the same converged state on all clients
Audit log must record the resolved order of conflicting same-cell edits
Must scale to 50k cells without broadcasting the whole sheet per edit

before_state.description: Every edit serializes the entire sheet to JSON and broadcasts it; the receiving client replaces its whole model. Row/column inserts don't rewrite formula references, and same-cell edits clobber.

issues: Whole-sheet broadcast is O(50k) per edit
Wholesale replace drops concurrent local edits
Recalc runs on possibly inconsistent intermediate state
insertRow shifts data but leaves formula refs pointing at old addresses -> #REF
No transform of a concurrent edit against the insert
No version vector to detect concurrency

reasoning: Naive fix: lock the sheet during an edit. Unacceptable — 8 analysts can't take turns typing. Considered a full CRDT spreadsheet (e.g., a tree of cells), rejected because spreadsheet formula reference rewriting on structural ops (insert row/col) is precisely where operational transformation is simpler and well-understood: you transform a pending op against a concurrent structural op. Chosen: per-cell operations with a version vector; structural ops (insert/delete row/col) carry a transform function that rewrites both addresses and formula references; the server linearizes ops per sheet and clients transform their pending ops against the authoritative stream. Cross-layer trace: UI emits a granular op {type, addr|range, value}; client tags it with its version vector and buffers it; server assigns a global sequence and broadcasts; each client transforms buffered local ops against incoming ops (including reference rewrite for structural ops) before applying; recalc runs only after convergence; audit log stores the linearized op order; tests assert formula refs survive concurrent inserts.

after_state.description: Granular cell ops with version vectors; server linearizes per sheet; clients transform pending ops; structural ops rewrite formula references; deterministic recalc after convergence.

files.purpose: Defines granular operations carrying a version vector so the server and clients can detect concurrency and order edits.
Rewrites a pending cell op (address AND embedded formula references) so it remains correct after a concurrent structural insert/delete — the core OT transform.
Assigns a single total order per sheet and persists each op as an immutable audit record of the resolved conflict order.
Optimistically applies local edits and transforms them against the authoritative stream so each client converges to identical state with valid formula references.

migration_strategy: Introduce op endpoint + sheet_ops table behind sheet_ot flag; legacy whole-sheet broadcast remains for unflagged sheets.
Shadow-run: emit granular ops alongside whole-sheet sync, compare converged state hashes; do not yet drive UI from ops.
Canary internal finance team's sheets on the OT path; assert recalc parity against the legacy snapshot.
Ramp to 100%; auto-fallback a sheet to whole-sheet sync if a transform invariant check fails (refs broken).
Remove the whole-sheet broadcast and legacy onRemote once parity holds for 2 weeks.
Archive but retain sheet_ops for audit; add a periodic snapshot to bound replay.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a sheet with B2 = '=A1+A2', rows 1..5 populated. Concurrent structural test: client X inserts a row at 1 while client Y edits A2; linearize both, assert on both clients B-formula now references the shifted addresses (no #REF) and the numeric result equals the model evaluated on the converged sheet. Side-effect assert: sheet_ops contains both ops in a single total order; replaying sheet_ops from empty reproduces the converged sheet hash. Same-cell test: two sets to C3 converge to the later-seq value and the audit shows both, loser recorded. Negative/chaos test: deliver remote ops out of order and duplicate one; assert idempotent apply (seq dedupe) and identical final hash. Recalc determinism: run recalc 100x on converged state, assert stable output.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the structural-op boundary, insertRow shifts cell data but the concurrent client's in-flight '=A1+A2' is never transformed, so after the shift A1/A2 now point at different data (or deleted cells), turning a correct formula into #REF or a silently wrong number that gets shown to a client.

title: Prevent collaborative code-editor desync by replacing line-diff patches with Monaco + Yjs binding and relative positions
business_context: A cloud IDE (devtools) supports pair-programming in a Monaco editor. The current sync sends unified line diffs; when both developers edit the same line, the diff fails to apply and the buffers silently diverge until someone reloads, losing work. About 15% of pairing sessions report a desync. Sessions average 2 developers but workshops hit 6 simultaneous editors on one file.
constraints: Cursor and selection of each peer must remain anchored to logical positions across remote edits
Must handle files up to 20k lines without re-sending the whole buffer
Language-server diagnostics must stay aligned with the converged text
Undo/redo must be per-user (your Ctrl+Z undoes only your edits)
Works behind a single WS proxy with reconnect/resume

before_state.description: Editor sends server-computed unified diffs on a debounce; applying a diff that overlaps a concurrent local edit throws and the client ignores it, diverging silently.

issues: applyPatch returns false on overlapping edits and the client just drops it -> divergence
setValue resets every peer's cursor and selection
Whole-text diff is expensive on 20k-line files
Absolute line/col cursors drift after remote inserts
No per-user undo isolation
Diagnostics keyed to absolute offsets desync after merge

reasoning: Naive fix: on patch failure, request a full resync. It stops silent divergence but causes constant full-buffer reloads under active co-editing and still resets cursors. Rejected a custom OT text engine: correct text OT (with cursor transformation and tombstones) is a multi-month effort and Yjs's Y.Text already provides it, tested at scale. Chosen: bind Monaco to a Y.Text via y-monaco; cursors use Yjs relative positions that survive concurrent edits; per-user undo via Y.UndoManager scoped to the local client id; diagnostics re-mapped through relative positions. Cross-layer trace: UI mounts MonacoBinding(yText, model, awareness); local edits become Y.Text deltas (incremental, not whole-buffer); WS provider syncs and resumes on reconnect; awareness carries each peer's relative-position cursor so it re-anchors after remote inserts; a diagnostics adapter converts LSP ranges to/from relative positions; tests assert convergence and cursor stability.

after_state.description: Monaco bound to Y.Text via y-monaco; relative-position cursors; per-user Y.UndoManager; diagnostics mapped through relative positions; incremental delta sync with resume.

files.purpose: Binds Monaco to Y.Text so edits merge per-character with stable cursors, and scopes undo to the local binding for per-user Ctrl+Z.
Keeps language-server diagnostics aligned with the converged text by anchoring them to Yjs relative positions instead of absolute offsets.
Provides resumable persistence so a reconnecting client loads the converged Y.Doc instead of triggering a whole-buffer reload.

migration_strategy: Add Y.Doc file_state persistence and the y-monaco binding behind editor_crdt flag; legacy diff sync stays for unflagged files.
Dogfood with the internal platform team; verify per-user undo and diagnostic alignment manually.
Canary 5% of pairing sessions; track desync-report rate vs control.
Ramp to 100%; if a session detects divergence (state hash mismatch on a heartbeat), it resyncs from Y.Doc, not a full text reload.
Remove diffSync once desync reports drop below 0.5% for 2 weeks.
Enable Yjs gc and snapshotting to bound memory on long workshop sessions.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 200-line TS file in a Y.Text. Convergence test: two clients edit the same line concurrently (one inserts at col 5, other deletes col 10-15); exchange updates; assert identical Y.Text on both and no dropped edit. Cursor stability test: peer A's cursor at line 50 col 3, peer B inserts 10 lines above; assert A's relative-position cursor still points to the same logical token. Diagnostics test: anchor a diagnostic at offset 120, apply a remote insert before it, assert resolveDiagnostics returns the shifted offset matching the same source range. Per-user undo test: A and B each type; A presses undo; assert only A's last edit is removed. Negative/chaos test: kill the WS mid-session, make 20 offline edits on each side, reconnect; assert convergence with zero lost characters.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the overlapping-edit boundary, applyPatch needs the exact base text its diff was computed against; when a concurrent local edit changed that line, the hunk context no longer matches, applyPatch returns false, the client silently drops the remote change, and the two buffers diverge with no signal until a manual reload.

title: Resolve concurrent reaction/emoji race on live messages by switching counter overwrite to a Yjs Y.Map per-user reaction set
business_context: A social platform shows live emoji reactions on posts during streams. The reaction count is stored as an integer and incremented with read-modify-write; under viral spikes (40k concurrent viewers reacting) increments are lost and the displayed count jitters down, which embarrasses creators and skews trending ranking.
constraints: No lost increments under 5k reactions/sec on a single post
Each user may react once per emoji (toggle); double-tap must not double-count
Counts converge across 8 fanout nodes with Redis
Existing posts with an integer reactions column must keep working
Trending pipeline reads must be eventually consistent within 2s

before_state.description: Reaction count is an integer updated by read-modify-write per tap; concurrent taps race and lose increments. No per-user identity, so toggles double-count.

issues: Read-modify-write loses concurrent increments
No per-user toggle identity -> double counting
Emits possibly-stale count to all viewers

reasoning: Naive fix: use an atomic SQL UPDATE count=count+1. Removes the lost-update race but still can't model toggles (a user un-reacting) and serializes 5k writes/sec on one hot row. Rejected Redis INCR alone — fast but loses per-user toggle identity and durable history. Chosen: model reactions as a Yjs Y.Map<emoji, Y.Map<userId, true>>; the count is derived (set size), so add/remove are idempotent and commutative; CRDT merges across nodes; a derived counter is periodically projected to Postgres for the trending pipeline. Cross-layer trace: UI toggles set membership in the Y.Map; updates fan out via Redis-backed Yjs provider; count = map size computed client-side (no integer to race); a debounced projector writes derived counts to the reactions table; legacy integer counts seed the initial map size; tests assert idempotent toggle and no lost increments.

after_state.description: Reactions are a Y.Map of per-user sets; counts derived from set size (idempotent, commutative). CRDT fanout across nodes; derived counts projected to Postgres for trending.

files.purpose: Models reactions as per-user CRDT sets so toggles are idempotent and the count is a derived set size that cannot lose increments.
Projects derived counts to Postgres on a debounce for the trending pipeline without putting an integer on the hot path.
Fans reaction CRDT updates across all 8 nodes via Redis so counts converge everywhere.

migration_strategy: Add the Y.Doc reaction store behind reactions_crdt flag; seed Y.Map from existing integer counts (anonymous filler members) on first load.
Shadow project derived counts to a shadow column; compare against legacy integer for drift.
Canary hot/trending posts to the CRDT path; verify counts only increase under load.
Ramp to 100%; projector writes authoritative counts, legacy increment path disabled.
Replace seeded anonymous members with real userIds as users re-react; drop legacy increment endpoint.
Keep integer column as the projected read model for trending; remove read-modify-write code.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a post with emoji counts. Load test: simulate 5,000 concurrent toggles from distinct userIds on two peers, merge, assert count == distinct users (no lost increments). Idempotency: same user toggles same emoji twice, assert net count delta 0. Side-effect: after project(), reactions table count equals Y.Map size per emoji. Cross-node: apply updates on node A and node B via Redis relay, assert identical sizes. Chaos test: drop Redis during a spike, buffer 1k toggles, restore, assert all converge with correct distinct-user counts.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the read-modify-write boundary, two taps both read count=100 and both write 101; one increment is lost, so under a 5k/sec spike the displayed count drifts well below the true number of reactors and can even appear to decrease.

title: Stop form-builder field loss in concurrent editing by moving array-index mutation to a Yjs Y.Array of field subdocuments
business_context: A B2B SaaS form builder lets multiple admins co-edit a form's fields. When two admins add or reorder fields simultaneously, fields disappear or swap because the app mutates a plain array by index and PATCHes the whole field list. Enterprise customers building 120-field onboarding forms lost work, escalating to the account team.
constraints: Concurrent add/remove/reorder must not lose fields
Each field has nested config (validation rules) editable concurrently
Form schema must stay JSON-Schema-exportable at all times
Up to 10 admins per form; sync over a single WS
Existing forms stored as a JSONB fields array must migrate

before_state.description: Fields are a JS array; edits splice by index and PATCH the entire array. Concurrent index splices on different clients overwrite each other.

issues: PATCH replaces whole array -> last write wins
Index splice races: two concurrent adds collide on index
Nested validation edits clobber when array replaced

reasoning: Naive fix: server merges arrays by field id on PATCH. Helps adds but reorder and nested validation edits still clobber because the merge has no causal info. Rejected sending granular array ops over a bespoke protocol — re-implements list CRDT semantics we'd maintain forever. Chosen: Y.Array of field Y.Maps (each field a subdocument), so adds/removes/reorders and nested validation edits are independent CRDT ops. Cross-layer trace: UI manipulates Y.Array (insert/delete/move) and field Y.Map for nested config; provider syncs deltas; a JSON-Schema exporter reads the Y.Doc on demand; legacy JSONB arrays import into a Y.Array; tests assert no field loss under concurrent structural edits.

after_state.description: Form is a Y.Array of field Y.Maps; structural and nested edits are independent CRDT ops; JSON-Schema exported from the Y.Doc.

files.purpose: Each field is a CRDT subdocument so concurrent adds, reorders, and nested rule edits merge without losing fields.
Derives an always-valid JSON Schema from the converged Y.Doc so export never sees a half-merged array.
Migrates legacy JSONB field arrays into a Y.Array preserving order and nested rules.

migration_strategy: Add Y.Doc form state behind form_crdt flag; legacy PATCH path stays.
Import a form to Y.Array on first open; dual-write JSONB for rollback.
Canary 5% of forms; track field-loss reports and export-validity checks.
Ramp to 100%; auto-rollback a form to JSONB if export schema ever invalid.
Stop dual-writing JSONB after 2 weeks clean; Y.Doc authoritative.
Remove array-splice store and PATCH endpoint.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 5-field form. Concurrency test: client A adds a field at index 2 while B reorders fields 0 and 4; merge; assert all original fields present plus the new one, no duplicates, deterministic order on both. Nested test: A and B edit different validation rules on the same field concurrently; assert both rules present. Side-effect: toJsonSchema produces a valid JSON Schema after every merge. Migration test: a legacy JSONB form imports to identical field list. Chaos test: drop and replay a structural delete out of order; assert field count correct and no resurrection of deleted field.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the concurrent-add boundary, both clients splice at index 2 into their local copy and PATCH the whole array; the server stores the last PATCH, so the field added by the first admin — never present in the second admin's array — is gone.

title: Scale Yjs document rooms across instances with a Redis-backed y-websocket adapter to fix cross-pod desync
business_context: A collaborative wiki uses y-websocket with in-memory rooms. After horizontal scaling to 6 Kubernetes pods, users connected to different pods editing the same page no longer see each other's changes because each pod holds an isolated in-memory Y.Doc. 30% of cross-team edits land on different pods, so 'live' collaboration silently isn't.
constraints: Two clients on different pods must converge in < 250ms
No sticky sessions (ALB round-robins WS upgrades)
Must persist the converged doc durably every few seconds
Survive a single pod crash without losing in-flight updates
Handle 2,000 concurrent rooms across the cluster

before_state.description: Default y-websocket utils keep each room's Y.Doc in process memory; updates broadcast only to sockets on the same pod.

issues: docs map is per-process; cross-pod clients never share a Y.Doc
No durable persistence beyond memory
Pod crash loses all in-flight updates for its rooms

reasoning: Naive fix: enable ALB sticky sessions so a room's clients land on one pod. Fragile — sticky cookies don't apply cleanly to WS upgrades, rebalancing/scale-down still splits rooms, and one hot room can overload a single pod. Rejected a single dedicated 'doc server' — reintroduces a SPOF and a scaling bottleneck. Chosen: a Redis pub/sub fanout layer plus Redis-stream persistence so any pod can host any room and updates replicate cluster-wide, with periodic snapshot to Postgres. Cross-layer trace: each pod subscribes to a Redis channel per active room; local updates publish to Redis; all pods apply incoming updates to their local Y.Doc and broadcast to their sockets; a Redis stream buffers updates for crash recovery; a snapshot job folds to Postgres; tests assert cross-pod convergence and crash recovery.

after_state.description: Redis pub/sub replicates Yjs updates across pods; Redis stream buffers for crash recovery; periodic Postgres snapshot. Any pod serves any room, no stickiness.

files.purpose: Replicates every local Yjs update to all pods via Redis pub/sub and appends to a Redis stream for crash-recovery replay; origin tag prevents echo loops.
On first client for a room on any pod, rebuilds the Y.Doc from the latest Postgres snapshot plus the Redis stream tail so no in-flight update is lost after a crash.
Durably snapshots the converged doc and trims the Redis stream to bound memory while keeping a recovery tail.

migration_strategy: Deploy Redis adapter behind ROOM_FANOUT=redis env, default off (in-memory) for safe rollback.
Enable on a staging cluster; verify cross-pod convergence with two pods.
Canary 10% of rooms (by hash) onto the Redis fanout in production.
Ramp to 100%; monitor cross-pod convergence latency and Redis stream lag.
Kill-test a pod under load; confirm rooms recover from snapshot+stream on other pods.
Remove in-memory-only path once stable for a week.
testing_strategy: Fixture: two server instances sharing a Redis. Cross-pod test: client1 on pod A edits, client2 on pod B; assert client2 receives the update and both Y.Docs converge within 250ms. Persistence test: after edits, run snapshot(), restart both pods, run loadRoom; assert restored doc equals pre-restart. Side-effect: doc_state row updated; Redis stream trimmed to <=1000. Crash test: kill pod A mid-edit (updates in stream not yet snapshotted), have pod B loadRoom; assert no lost updates. Echo test: assert an update originating from Redis is not re-published (no infinite loop).
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the pod boundary, the y-websocket docs map lives in one process's heap; a client whose WS upgrade was round-robined to a different pod edits a different in-memory Y.Doc instance, so neither side's updates ever reach the other and the room silently splits into two divergent documents.

title: Bound unbounded Yjs memory growth on long-lived rooms by adding gc, snapshotting, and tombstone compaction
business_context: A collaborative incident-response timeline tool keeps rooms open for the duration of a multi-day incident. Long rooms (some open 5+ days with 200k edits) grow the in-memory Y.Doc past 1.5GB, OOM-killing the pod and dropping every active responder mid-incident. SREs have lost timeline state during P1s.
constraints: A room open 7 days with 500k ops must stay under 256MB resident
Compaction must not break clients mid-session (no full reload)
Deleted content must remain recoverable for 24h for incident audit
Reconnecting clients must resume from compacted state
No global stop-the-world pause > 50ms

before_state.description: Y.Doc created with gc disabled and never snapshotted; deleted content tombstones accumulate forever, growing memory unbounded.

issues: gc:false keeps every deleted item forever
No snapshot -> full op log replayed and held in memory
No tombstone compaction -> unbounded growth -> OOM

reasoning: Naive fix: enable gc:true. It collects garbage but loses deleted content needed for 24h audit, and alone doesn't bound the structure on a 7-day room. Rejected periodically recreating the doc and forcing all clients to reload — that drops responders mid-incident. Chosen: keep gc on with a snapshot-before-gc pipeline: take a Y.snapshot (cheap, audit-recoverable) every interval, persist the encoded state, then let gc reclaim; a sliding 24h snapshot retention satisfies audit; reconnecting clients resume from the latest compacted state. Cross-layer trace: a timer captures Y.snapshot and encodeStateAsUpdate to Postgres; gc runs incrementally; old snapshots beyond 24h are pruned; reconnect loads the compacted update; tests assert memory bound and recoverability.

after_state.description: gc enabled with periodic Y.snapshot audit checkpoints, compacted state persisted, 24h snapshot retention, resume-from-compacted on reconnect. Memory bounded.

files.purpose: Captures an audit-recoverable snapshot before gc reclaims tombstones and keeps 24h of checkpoints for incident review.
Reconstructs the timeline as it was at a past checkpoint so deleted entries are recoverable for 24h audit.
Runs gc-enabled docs with a 5-minute checkpoint+prune loop, bounding memory without dropping live responders.

migration_strategy: Enable gc:true on new rooms behind room_gc flag; keep existing rooms on gc:false.
Add checkpoint pipeline in shadow (write snapshots, don't prune) to validate recoverability.
Canary gc on a subset of new incident rooms; track RSS over multi-day soak.
Enable 24h pruning once recovery tests pass; ramp to all rooms.
Migrate existing long rooms by checkpointing then recreating with gc:true at a quiet window.
Remove gc:false path.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a room with a Y.Text. Memory test: apply 200k insert+delete ops with gc:true and periodic checkpoint; assert RSS stays under threshold and stable (no monotonic growth). Recoverability test: delete a timeline entry, checkpoint, then deletedAt(before-delete) returns the entry. Retention test: advance time 25h, pruneOld removes old snapshots; recovery beyond 24h returns nothing. Side-effect: room_snapshots row count bounded. Chaos test: trigger gc mid-edit storm; assert no pause > 50ms (measure) and clients stay converged.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the long-lived-room boundary, gc:false keeps a tombstone for every one of 500k deleted items plus the full op log in heap; over days the Y.Doc balloons past the pod's memory limit and the OOM killer terminates the process, disconnecting every responder at once.

title: Authorize and authenticate Yjs WebSocket rooms so users cannot read or mutate docs they lack access to
business_context: A healthcare care-plan editor uses Yjs over WebSocket. The room name equals the plan id and the WS endpoint has no auth, so anyone who guesses a plan id can connect and read/edit a patient's care plan. A pentest flagged this as a HIPAA-critical IDOR. 40k care plans, each restricted to a specific care team.
constraints: Every WS connection must carry a verified JWT bound to the user
Per-room authorization checked against the care team before any sync
A token revoked mid-session must terminate the socket within 30s
No PHI in logs or room names
Read-only collaborators must not be able to send updates

before_state.description: WS upgrade accepts any connection; room = plan id; no token verification or per-room authz; updates accepted from anyone.

issues: No JWT verification on upgrade
No per-room authorization -> IDOR on plan ids
Read-only users can send updates

reasoning: Naive fix: check a JWT in the upgrade query string and accept. Insufficient — it omits per-room authz (any valid user could open any plan), can't revoke mid-session, and can't enforce read-only. Rejected pushing authz into the client (hide the editor) — trivially bypassable. Chosen: verify JWT on upgrade, resolve user, check care-team membership and role for the requested plan, attach a permission to the socket, gate inbound Yjs messages on write permission, and run a revocation heartbeat that closes sockets whose token/grant was revoked. Cross-layer trace: upgrade verifies JWT and authorizes the room; a message interceptor drops sync-step-2/update frames from read-only sockets while allowing awareness; a 30s heartbeat re-checks grant validity in Redis; audit logs connection/authz without PHI; tests assert IDOR blocked, read-only enforced, revocation closes socket.

after_state.description: JWT verified on upgrade, per-room care-team authorization, write-gating for read-only roles, 30s revocation heartbeat, PHI-free audit.

files.purpose: Verifies the JWT and authorizes care-team access to the specific plan before any Yjs sync, closing the IDOR.
Drops/forbids document update frames from read-only sockets so viewers cannot mutate the care plan.
Closes sockets within 30s when a token expires or a user is revoked mid-session.

migration_strategy: Deploy auth on upgrade in audit-only mode (log would-be-denials, still allow) behind ws_authz flag.
Review audit for false denials; fix authz edge cases (cross-team consults).
Flip to enforcing for internal accounts; verify read-only gating and revocation.
Ramp to 100% over a week; clients must attach token (older clients prompted to update).
Enable revocation heartbeat; integrate with the auth service's revocation set.
Remove unauthenticated upgrade path.
testing_strategy: Fixture: plan P owned by team T; userA in T (write), userB in T (read), userC not in T. IDOR test: userC connects to room P, assert 403 and no Y.Doc bytes sent. Read-only test: userB sends a sync update frame, assert socket closed 4003 and Y.Doc unchanged. Side-effect: an audit row logs userId+planId+decision with no PHI. Revocation test: add userA to revoked set, advance 30s, assert socket closed 4001. Negative/chaos test: forged JWT (bad signature) rejected 401; expired token mid-session closed by heartbeat.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the upgrade boundary, a token-only check still lets any authenticated user open room=<any planId>, because the room name is the only thing distinguishing documents and nothing verifies the user belongs to that plan's care team — the IDOR survives.

title: Make collaborative undo/redo correct under concurrency by scoping Y.UndoManager and excluding remote origins
business_context: A collaborative diagram tool's global undo button undoes the last change regardless of who made it, so pressing Ctrl+Z reverts a teammate's work and creates 'undo wars'. The current implementation snapshots the whole document and pops a global stack. Teams of 4 designers routinely corrupt diagrams this way; it's the #2 complaint.
constraints: Ctrl+Z must only undo the local user's own operations
Undo must remain correct after concurrent remote edits interleave
Undo must not resurrect content another user deleted after your op
Stack must survive reconnect within the session
Works on diagrams with 5k shapes

before_state.description: A global array of full-document JSON snapshots; undo restores the previous snapshot wholesale, reverting everyone's changes and resetting cursors.

issues: Global snapshots revert ALL users' work, not just yours
Wholesale restore overwrites concurrent remote edits
O(doc) memory per undo step

reasoning: Naive fix: tag each snapshot with the actor and skip undos that aren't yours. Still wrong: a whole-doc restore overwrites concurrent remote edits and can resurrect deleted content. Rejected an op-inverse log we maintain by hand — re-implements exactly what Y.UndoManager does, including transform against concurrent ops. Chosen: a per-user Y.UndoManager that tracks only operations whose origin is the local client, so undo inverts just the local ops and Yjs transforms the inverse against concurrent remote changes; redo symmetric. Cross-layer trace: local edits carry a local origin; UndoManager constructed with trackedOrigins={localOrigin}; remote provider updates use a different origin and are excluded; undo/redo emit Yjs ops that merge normally; tests assert undo affects only local ops even after interleaved remote edits.

after_state.description: Per-user Y.UndoManager tracking only local-origin ops; undo/redo emit transform-aware inverse ops; remote edits excluded; bounded memory.

files.purpose: Scopes undo/redo to operations originating from the local client only, so Ctrl+Z never reverts a teammate's edit.
Tags every local mutation with LOCAL_ORIGIN so the UndoManager picks it up while remote (provider-origin) updates are excluded.
Remote updates arrive with the provider's origin, so they are naturally excluded from the local undo stack.

migration_strategy: Introduce Y.UndoManager behind undo_v2 flag; legacy snapshot undo remains for unflagged sessions.
Dogfood: verify undo only affects local ops with 3 manual editors.
Canary 10% of sessions; track 'undo war' complaint rate.
Ramp to 100%; auto-fallback to disabling undo (rather than global) if UndoManager errors.
Remove snapshot history once complaints drop and stacks behave on reconnect.
Tune captureTimeout to group rapid local edits into single undo steps.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a diagram Y.Doc with shapes S1,S2. Isolation test: local user moves S1, remote user moves S2, local presses undo; assert S1 reverts and S2 stays at remote position. Interleave test: local moves S1, remote deletes S1; local undo; assert no crash and S1 not resurrected (transform handles deleted target). Redo test: undo then redo restores local change only. Side-effect: undoStack length reflects only local transactions. Chaos test: reconnect mid-session with pending undo stack; assert undo still inverts the correct local ops after re-sync.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the multi-user boundary, the global snapshot stack has no concept of authorship or concurrency, so popping a snapshot restores a whole-document state that predates and therefore erases every other user's concurrent edits — turning one person's Ctrl+Z into data loss for the whole team.

title: Fix collaborative cursor jitter and identity loss by encoding cursors as Yjs relative positions with stable user colors
business_context: A collaborative SQL query editor shows teammates' cursors, but they jump to the wrong character after anyone types above them, and a user's color reshuffles on every reconnect, making it impossible to tell who is who during incident debugging sessions with 6 analysts in one query.
constraints: A remote cursor must stay on the same logical character after local/remote inserts
A user's color must be stable across reconnects within a session
Selections (ranges) must also re-anchor, not just carets
Cursor updates throttled to <= 30/s
Works with up to 12 cursors visible

before_state.description: Cursors broadcast as absolute character offsets and a random color per connection; offsets drift after inserts and colors reshuffle on reconnect.

files.content:
export function sendCursor(offset:number){ ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type:'cursor', offset, color: '#'+Math.floor(Math.random()*16777215).toString(16) })); }
export function onRemoteCursor(m:any){ drawCaret(m.offset, m.color); } // absolute offset drifts after inserts
issues: Absolute offset is wrong after any insertion before it
Random color per message -> identity flicker
Only caret, no selection range support

reasoning: Naive fix: recompute remote offsets by tracking net inserted length. Brittle — concurrent multi-region edits make the bookkeeping wrong, and it still flickers colors. Rejected server-side offset transformation — duplicates Yjs relative-position machinery. Chosen: store cursors in Yjs awareness as relative positions (anchor+head) that Yjs maps back to absolute indices after any edit, and derive a deterministic color from a stable userId hash. Cross-layer trace: UI on selection-change writes Y.createRelativePositionFromTypeIndex for anchor and head into awareness, throttled by rAF; peers convert relative->absolute via createAbsolutePositionFromRelativePosition on every doc/awareness change; color = hash(userId); tests assert cursor stability under inserts and color stability across reconnect.

after_state.description: Cursors stored as Yjs relative positions (anchor+head) in awareness; mapped to absolute on render; deterministic per-user color; throttled updates; selection ranges supported.

files.purpose: Encodes caret and selection as relative positions so they re-anchor to the correct logical characters after any concurrent edit.

files.content:
const PALETTE=['#e6194b','#3cb44b','#4363d8','#f58231','#911eb4','#46f0f0','#f032e6','#bcf60c'];
export function colorFor(userId:string){ let h=0; for(const c of userId) h=(h*31+c.charCodeAt(0))>>>0; return PALETTE[h%PALETTE.length]; }
files.purpose: Derives a stable color from a hash of the userId so identity is consistent across reconnects.
Renders each peer's selection at its current absolute position with a stable per-user color, skipping the local client.

migration_strategy: Add relative-position cursors in awareness behind cursor_v2 flag; legacy absolute path stays.
Canary internal analysts; verify cursors don't drift and colors persist across reconnect.
Ramp to 100%; keep legacy renderer behind flag for instant rollback.
Remove absolute-offset cursor code after a week clean.
Tune rAF throttle and add selection highlight rendering.
Backfill color palette to brand colors.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a Y.Text with a SQL query, peer cursor at offset 40. Stability test: insert 10 chars before offset 40; assert resolved peer cursor now at 50 (same logical token). Selection test: peer selects offsets 10-20, local inserts 5 chars at 5; assert selection resolves to 15-25. Color test: same userId across two reconnects yields identical color; two different users get different colors. Throttle test: 100 selection changes in one frame -> one awareness write. Chaos test: delete the text the peer's cursor was on; assert resolveSelection returns null and the cursor is hidden rather than mispositioned.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the insert-before-cursor boundary, an absolute offset of 40 still says 40 after someone inserts 10 characters at the start, so the remote caret renders 10 characters too early — and because the color is random per message, the analysts can't even tell whose misplaced cursor it is.

title: Eliminate awareness storm meltdown by batching and rate-limiting Yjs awareness updates on a 500-user webinar canvas
business_context: An online-education platform runs live webinars where up to 500 attendees move cursors and raise reactions on a shared canvas. Each awareness change is broadcast O(N) to every peer, so 500 users produce up to 250k messages/sec, melting client CPUs and the WS gateway. The canvas freezes and attendees drop.
constraints: Support 500 concurrent awareness participants per room
Per-client outbound awareness <= 10 msgs/sec regardless of activity
Server fanout must not exceed 5k msgs/sec/room
Cursors may be coarse (sampled) but reactions must not be dropped
Degrades gracefully: presenters always prioritized

before_state.description: Every awareness change immediately broadcasts to all peers with no batching or rate limiting; N participants => O(N^2) message volume.

issues: No batching: every tiny cursor move fans out immediately
O(N) per change x N changers => O(N^2) total
Reactions compete with cursor spam and get dropped under load

reasoning: Naive fix: throttle each client's awareness to 10/s on the client. Helps but the server still does O(N) fanout per accepted message and can't prioritize reactions over cursors. Rejected dropping awareness entirely above a threshold — kills the live feel. Chosen: a server-side coalescing window that batches awareness deltas per room into a single encoded frame at a fixed tick, separates a guaranteed reaction lane from a sampled cursor lane, and applies per-client inbound rate limits. Cross-layer trace: clients rate-limit outbound cursors (rAF + 10/s cap); the server accumulates awareness changes and flushes a merged frame every 100ms (cursor lane sampled to most-recent-per-client, reaction lane never dropped); presenters bypass sampling; tests assert message-rate ceilings and zero reaction loss.

after_state.description: Server-side awareness coalescing on a 100ms tick, separate guaranteed reaction lane vs sampled cursor lane, per-client inbound rate limits, presenter prioritization.

files.purpose: Coalesces all awareness changes in a room into a single broadcast every 100ms, collapsing O(N^2) traffic to O(N) per tick.
Guarantees reactions are delivered (batched, never dropped) on a lane separate from sampled cursor traffic.
Token-bucket rate-limits inbound awareness per client (presenters get a higher cap) to protect the gateway.

migration_strategy: Deploy coalescer + lanes behind awareness_coalesce flag at 100ms tick; legacy immediate relay for unflagged rooms.
Load-test a synthetic 500-user room; verify msg-rate ceilings.
Canary real webinars under 200 attendees; watch client CPU and dropped-frame metrics.
Ramp to all rooms; tune tick (100->150ms) if gateway still hot.
Enable presenter prioritization and inbound limiter cluster-wide.
Remove immediate relay path.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a simulated room with 500 clients emitting cursor moves + occasional reactions. Rate test: assert outbound frames per room <= ~10/s (100ms tick) and per-client inbound capped at 10/s. Reaction-integrity test: emit 1,000 reactions during a cursor storm; assert all 1,000 delivered (reaction lane never drops). Sampling test: a client emits 100 cursor updates in one tick; assert only the latest is in the flushed frame. Side-effect: limiter rejects over-cap clients without disconnecting them. Chaos test: spike to 500 simultaneous joins; assert gateway CPU stays bounded and presenters' cursors are never sampled away.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the fanout boundary, immediate per-change broadcast means each of N participants' cursor moves is sent to N peers, so 500 active users generate up to N*N = 250,000 messages per second, saturating client event loops and the gateway until the canvas freezes.

title: Add deterministic conflict-resolution UI for concurrent scalar edits using Automerge conflicts() surfacing
business_context: A CRM lets reps co-edit a deal record. The 'deal stage' and 'amount' fields are single values; when two reps set them concurrently the app silently keeps one and discards the other, causing revenue forecasts to be wrong. Finance flagged a $2.1M forecast discrepancy traced to a silent overwrite. ~300 deals/day have concurrent edits.
constraints: Concurrent scalar edits must converge deterministically across all clients
Both conflicting values must be visible to the user for resolution
Resolution choice must be auditable (who, when, kept which)
Must not block editing other fields during a conflict
Existing deals as flat rows must migrate

before_state.description: Scalar fields written via last-write-wins PATCH; loser silently discarded with no surfacing or audit.

files.content: deal.patch('/deals/:id',async(req,res)=>{ await db.query('UPDATE deals SET stage=$1, amount=$2 WHERE id=$3',[req.body.stage,req.body.amount,req.params.id]); res.json({ok:true}); });
issues: Last-write-wins silently discards a concurrent value
No surfacing of the conflict to users
No audit of which value was kept

reasoning: Naive fix: optimistic locking with version reject. Turns every concurrent edit into a 409 the rep must redo, and still doesn't show what the other value was. Rejected always merging numerically (e.g., max amount) — wrong for stage and hides intent. Chosen: Automerge for the record so concurrent scalar writes converge to a deterministic winner AND retain the conflicting value via getConflicts, which the UI surfaces for explicit resolution, logged for audit. Cross-layer trace: UI mutates an Automerge doc; on render it reads getConflicts(doc,field) and shows a chooser when >1 value; a resolve action writes the chosen value as a new change (clearing the conflict) and logs it; legacy rows import via Automerge.from; tests assert deterministic convergence + conflict surfacing + audit.

after_state.description: Automerge deal record; deterministic convergence with getConflicts surfacing both values; explicit resolution writes a new change and audit row.

files.purpose: Wraps scalar writes in Automerge changes so concurrency is detectable via getConflicts rather than silently lost.
Shows every concurrent value for a field and lets the rep pick the correct one instead of guessing what was lost.
Persists changes, detects scalar conflicts deterministically, and records them for audit and finance review.

migration_strategy: Add deal_doc + deal_conflicts tables behind crm_crdt flag; legacy PATCH stays.
Import a deal to Automerge on first edit; dual-write flat columns for rollback and reporting.
Canary one sales region; verify conflict chooser appears and forecasts reconcile.
Ramp to all regions; auto-fallback to flat columns if applyChanges errors.
Stop dual-writing flat columns after forecast discrepancies drop to zero for a month.
Build a finance report off deal_conflicts to verify no silent loss.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a deal with amount=100000. Convergence test: repA sets amount=120000, repB sets amount=90000 concurrently; apply both orders; assert identical deterministic winner on all clients AND getConflicts returns both values. Side-effect: a deal_conflicts row records both values + actor. Resolution test: rep picks 120000; assert a new change clears the conflict and the chooser disappears. Non-blocking test: while amount is in conflict, editing notes succeeds. Migration test: a flat deal imports with equal values. Chaos test: apply the same change twice; assert idempotent and no duplicate conflict row.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the concurrent-scalar boundary, the last PATCH overwrites the deals row with one rep's amount, and because nothing records the other rep's value, the forecast silently uses a number that one of the two reps never intended and no one can tell a value was lost.

title: Persist Yjs documents durably with debounced writes and crash-safe WAL instead of losing edits on pod restart
business_context: A collaborative meeting-notes app holds the live Y.Doc only in WS-server memory and writes to Postgres on socket close. When a pod is rolled during a deploy (multiple times/day) all in-memory docs vanish before the close handler runs, losing the last few minutes of notes for active meetings. Users lost notes during a board meeting.
constraints: At most 5s of edits may be lost on an ungraceful pod kill
Persist must not block the edit hot path
Durable store survives pod crash and deploy rollouts
Concurrent persistence from two pods for one doc must not corrupt state
Handle 3,000 active docs without write amplification meltdown

before_state.description: Y.Doc lives in memory; persistence only fires on 'close'. SIGTERM during deploy kills the process before close, losing recent edits.

issues: Only persists on close -> deploy SIGTERM loses edits
Whole-state write per close, no incremental durability
No crash recovery; in-memory only

reasoning: Naive fix: persist on every update. Correct durability but write amplification — 3k docs * keystrokes overwhelms Postgres. Rejected persisting only on a long interval — widens the loss window beyond 5s. Chosen: append incremental updates to a fast WAL (Redis stream or append-only table) synchronously-ish, debounce-fold into a full snapshot, and add a SIGTERM handler that flushes; recovery replays snapshot+WAL. Cross-layer trace: every doc update appends to a WAL keyed by doc (cheap append, bounds loss to one tick); a 5s debounce folds WAL into a snapshot and trims; a SIGTERM hook flushes all dirty docs before exit; on room load, snapshot+WAL replay rebuilds; a fencing token prevents two pods double-snapshotting; tests assert <=5s loss on kill and no corruption.

after_state.description: Incremental WAL append per update + debounced snapshot fold + SIGTERM flush + replay recovery + fencing to avoid double-write.

files.purpose: Appends each update to a Redis stream WAL so durability lag is one update, and replays the WAL on recovery.
Debounced fold of WAL into a snapshot guarded by a fencing token so a slower pod cannot overwrite a newer snapshot.
Flushes all dirty docs on SIGTERM so a graceful deploy loses nothing; ungraceful kills still bounded by the WAL.

migration_strategy: Add WAL append + snapshot fold behind durable_wal flag; legacy close-only persist remains.
Shadow: write WAL without changing read path; verify replay reproduces live state.
Canary: enable for a subset of docs; run a deploy and confirm zero data loss.
Ramp to 100%; add SIGTERM flush and fencing.
Run a chaos deploy (kill -9) and confirm <=5s loss bound.
Remove close-only persist.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a notes doc with active edits. Durability test: apply edits, then SIGKILL the pod (no SIGTERM), recover via snapshot+WAL replay; assert at most the last unflushed update lost (<=5s). Graceful test: SIGTERM flushes; assert zero loss. Fencing test: two pods fold concurrently with different fence tokens; assert only the higher fence persists, no corruption. Side-effect: WAL trimmed after fold; notes.fence monotonic. Load test: 3k docs editing; assert Postgres write rate bounded by the fold interval, not keystrokes. Chaos test: Redis blip during WAL append; assert update retried and not lost.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the deploy boundary, Kubernetes sends SIGTERM and the process exits (or is SIGKILLed after grace) before any socket 'close' fires, so the close-only persist never runs and the last minutes of every active meeting's Y.Doc — held only in heap — are gone.

title: Sync a collaborative Tiptap editor to a Vue 3 app with Hocuspocus, fixing reactivity tearing on remote updates
business_context: A Vue 3 + Nuxt content-marketing tool embeds a Tiptap editor. The team bolted Yjs on by copying Y.Text into a Vue ref and writing back on change, which double-applies remote updates and tears the editor (duplicated paragraphs, cursor jumps). Editors lose formatting during co-editing of campaign briefs. 800 daily editors, 2-3 per brief.
constraints: Remote Yjs updates must not loop back into Y.Doc via Vue reactivity
Editor must stay mounted across remote edits (no remount/flicker)
Must use Hocuspocus server with auth hook
SSR (Nuxt) must not initialize the WS provider on the server
Works with Tiptap collaboration + collaboration-cursor extensions

before_state.description: Y.Text mirrored into a Vue ref and written back in a watcher; remote updates trigger the watcher which re-writes the Y.Doc, causing echo and tearing.

files.content:
<script setup>
import * as Y from 'yjs'; import { ref, watch } from 'vue';
const ydoc=new Y.Doc(); const ytext=ydoc.getText('content');
const model=ref(ytext.toString());
ytext.observe(()=>{ model.value=ytext.toString(); }); // remote -> ref
watch(model,(v)=>{ ytext.delete(0,ytext.length); ytext.insert(0,v); }); // ref -> Y.Doc, echoes!
</script>
<template><textarea v-model='model' /></template>
issues: watch writes back to Y.Doc on remote-driven ref change -> echo loop
delete+insert whole text destroys cursors and collapses concurrent edits
No Tiptap binding; plain textarea can't do rich text or cursors

reasoning: Naive fix: add a guard flag to skip the watcher during remote updates. Fragile and still uses whole-text delete+insert that loses concurrency. Rejected polling diff between ref and Y.Text — reintroduces the merge problem CRDTs solve. Chosen: drop the mirror entirely and let Tiptap's Collaboration extension bind directly to the Y.Doc via @tiptap/extension-collaboration + HocuspocusProvider, with the provider created only on client (Nuxt onMounted / process.client), so Vue reactivity never touches the Y.Doc. Cross-layer trace: editor is a Tiptap instance whose document is the Y.Doc fragment; HocuspocusProvider handles sync + awareness; an auth hook on the Hocuspocus server authorizes the room; SSR skips provider init; tests assert no echo and convergence.

after_state.description: Tiptap Collaboration extension bound directly to the Y.Doc via HocuspocusProvider; client-only init; auth hook; no Vue mirror, no echo.

files.content:
<script setup>
import { onMounted, onBeforeUnmount, shallowRef } from 'vue';
import * as Y from 'yjs';
import { HocuspocusProvider } from '@hocuspocus/provider';
import { Editor } from '@tiptap/vue-3';
import StarterKit from '@tiptap/starter-kit';
import Collaboration from '@tiptap/extension-collaboration';
import CollaborationCursor from '@tiptap/extension-collaboration-cursor';
const props=defineProps({ docId:String, user:Object });
const editor=shallowRef(null); let provider, ydoc;
onMounted(()=>{ // client only: never runs during SSR
  ydoc=new Y.Doc();
  provider=new HocuspocusProvider({ url: WS_URL, name: props.docId, document: ydoc, token: getToken() });
  editor.value=new Editor({ extensions:[ StarterKit.configure({ history:false }), Collaboration.configure({ document: ydoc }), CollaborationCursor.configure({ provider, user: props.user }) ] });
});
onBeforeUnmount(()=>{ editor.value?.destroy(); provider?.destroy(); ydoc?.destroy(); });
</script>
<template><EditorContent v-if='editor' :editor='editor' /></template>
files.purpose: Binds Tiptap directly to the Y.Doc so Vue reactivity never mirrors or writes back the CRDT, eliminating the echo/tearing loop; provider init is client-only for Nuxt SSR.
Hocuspocus server authorizes each room via onAuthenticate and persists Y.Doc state through the Database extension.

files.content: export function getToken(){ return useCookie('access_token').value || ''; }
files.purpose: Supplies the auth token to the provider so the server's onAuthenticate hook can authorize the brief.

migration_strategy: Stand up Hocuspocus server with Database extension behind collab_tiptap flag; legacy mirror editor stays.
Enable for internal content team; verify no tearing and cursors render.
Canary 10% of briefs; track formatting-loss reports and remount flicker.
Ramp to 100%; auto-fallback to read-only legacy view if provider fails to connect.
Remove the mirror-watcher editor.
Tune Hocuspocus debounce + add awareness rate limiting.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a Tiptap Y.Doc with two paragraphs. Echo test: apply a remote update; assert the Y.Doc receives no additional local write (no duplicate paragraph) and the editor stays mounted. Convergence test: two providers edit concurrently; assert identical ProseMirror JSON. SSR test: render the component on the server; assert no provider/WS instantiated (process.client guard). Auth test: invalid token rejected by onAuthenticate, no doc served. Side-effect: briefs.state persisted via store. Chaos test: disconnect/reconnect; assert content converges and cursors reappear with stable colors.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the reactivity boundary, the observe->ref->watch->Y.Text cycle means a remote update updates the ref, which fires the watcher, which deletes and re-inserts the entire Y.Text — echoing a synthetic local edit that collides with the genuine remote one and duplicates paragraphs while resetting every cursor.

title: Throttle and snapshot a collaborative game lobby's shared state CRDT to fit a 60Hz tick without flooding clients
business_context: A multiplayer party-game's pre-match lobby lets 16 players concurrently toggle ready, pick characters, and edit team names on shared state synced via a CRDT. Naively every CRDT change broadcasts immediately, so rapid ready-toggling produces hundreds of frames/sec that desync the lobby UI and spike mobile data. Players see flickering ready states.
constraints: Lobby broadcast must align to the 60Hz client tick (no more than ~60 updates/sec)
Character pick must be exclusive: two players can't hold the same character
Ready state must be eventually consistent within 100ms
Mobile clients must not exceed 50KB/s of lobby traffic
Reconnect within 5s restores lobby slot

before_state.description: Each CRDT mutation broadcasts an immediate update; no tick alignment, no exclusivity for character picks (two players can both 'hold' one).

issues: Immediate broadcast per toggle floods at 60Hz x 16 players
No exclusivity: two players map to same charId
No tick batching -> UI flicker + mobile data spike

reasoning: Naive fix: client-side debounce on toggles. Reduces spam but two clients can still claim the same character (no exclusivity), and per-client debounce doesn't align broadcasts to the render tick. Rejected a central authoritative lobby server doing all mutations — adds latency and a SPOF for a feature CRDTs handle if we add a claim protocol. Chosen: keep the CRDT but (a) coalesce outbound updates to the 60Hz tick, (b) implement character exclusivity via a first-writer-wins claim using a Y.Map<charId, playerId> with a deterministic tie-break so concurrent claims resolve identically everywhere. Cross-layer trace: mutations write to the Y.Doc; a tick loop flushes a merged awareness/update frame at 60Hz; character claim writes to charId->player map and on conflict the lower playerId wins deterministically, the loser auto-rebound to a free slot; tests assert exclusivity and tick rate.

after_state.description: 60Hz-tick-coalesced broadcast; deterministic first-writer-wins character exclusivity with auto-rebind; bounded mobile traffic.

files.purpose: Resolves concurrent character claims deterministically (lower playerId wins) so every client converges to the same exclusive assignment.
Coalesces all CRDT updates within a frame and emits one merged update per 60Hz tick, eliminating flicker and bounding bandwidth.
Automatically reassigns a player who lost a contested character to the next free slot so no one is left without a character.

migration_strategy: Add tick broadcaster + claim map behind lobby_v2 flag; legacy immediate broadcast stays.
Canary internal playtests; verify exclusivity and 60Hz cap on captured traffic.
Ramp to all lobbies; monitor mobile bandwidth and ready-flicker reports.
Enable auto-rebind; verify no player stuck character-less.
Remove immediate-broadcast path.
Tune tie-break and rebind ordering for fairness.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 4-player lobby Y.Doc. Exclusivity test: players P1 and P2 both claim char 'mage' concurrently on two peers; merge; assert exactly one holds 'mage' (lower id) on both peers and the loser auto-rebinds to a free char. Tick test: toggle ready 200 times in one second; assert <=60 broadcast frames emitted and the final ready state is correct. Bandwidth test: measure bytes/sec under a 16-player toggle storm; assert <50KB/s. Side-effect: charClaims has no duplicate values. Chaos test: a player disconnects mid-claim and reconnects within 5s; assert their slot restored and exclusivity preserved.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the concurrent-claim boundary, two players write picks[player]=charId for the same charId, and because the map is keyed by player not character, both writes succeed and the lobby shows two players holding the same character with no rule to break the tie identically across clients.

title: Sync IoT device-config edits from multiple operators with Automerge to survive intermittent gateway connectivity
business_context: An industrial-IoT platform lets multiple operators edit a device fleet's configuration (thresholds, schedules) from a dashboard, while edge gateways also adjust config locally during network partitions. Current sync is server-LWW, so an operator's threshold change made during a partition is silently overwritten when the gateway reconnects, causing a furnace to run on a stale setpoint. 12k gateways, partitions up to 20 min.
constraints: Config edits during a partition must merge, not be overwritten on reconnect
Numeric thresholds need an explicit conflict policy (operator intent > automatic)
Sync payload over constrained links (LTE-M) must be minimal
Edge gateway runs the same CRDT logic in a constrained runtime
Audit every config change with origin (operator vs gateway)

before_state.description: Server stores config as a row; on gateway reconnect it pushes its whole config and overwrites the server, discarding operator edits made during the partition.

files.content: app.post('/devices/:id/config',async(req,res)=>{ await db.query('UPDATE device_config SET cfg=$1 WHERE device_id=$2',[req.body.cfg,req.params.id]); res.json({ok:true}); });
issues: Whole-config overwrite -> operator edits during partition lost
No merge of concurrent operator vs gateway changes
No origin audit for safety-critical setpoints

reasoning: Naive fix: timestamp config and apply newest. Clocks on edge gateways drift and an operator's deliberate change can lose to a routine gateway write. Rejected server-only authority — impossible during a 20-min partition where the gateway must act autonomously. Chosen: Automerge config doc replicated to gateway and server; concurrent edits merge with a domain conflict policy (operator-origin changes win ties over gateway-origin) encoded as application logic over Automerge conflicts; delta sync minimizes LTE-M payload.
