Cross-layer trace: operators and gateways both mutate an Automerge doc tagging origin; reconnect exchanges only missing changes; a resolver reads getConflicts on safety fields and applies the operator-wins policy as an explicit change (auditable); origins logged; tests assert partition merge and policy.

after_state.description: Automerge config doc on server+gateway; delta sync; explicit operator-wins conflict policy on safety fields; origin-tagged audit.

files.purpose: Tags each safety-field change with its origin so the resolver can apply an operator-priority policy deterministically.
Applies the explicit operator-wins policy on conflicting setpoints as a new auditable change, clearing the conflict safely.
Exchanges only missing change blocks to minimize traffic over constrained LTE-M links.

migration_strategy: Deploy Automerge config doc behind iot_crdt flag on server; gateways keep LWW until firmware update.
Update gateway firmware in waves to run the same CRDT; dual-store legacy cfg for rollback.
Canary one site; force a partition and verify operator edits survive reconnect.
Ramp by region; resolver applies operator-priority and logs origins.
Disable legacy whole-config overwrite once merge verified across partitions.
Audit safety-field resolutions monthly.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a device config with setpoint=400. Partition test: operator sets 380 (origin operator) on server while gateway sets 420 (origin gateway) offline; reconnect and delta-sync; assert merged doc converges and resolve() yields 380 (operator wins) on both. Side-effect: an audit/origin entry shows both changes and the resolution. Payload test: assert delta sync transmits only the missing changes, not the whole doc. Determinism: apply in both orders -> same result. Chaos test: truncated change over LTE-M rejected without corrupting stored doc; gateway retries.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the reconnect boundary, the gateway POSTs its whole config and the server UPDATE replaces the row, so the operator's setpoint change made during the partition — never present in the gateway's config — is silently discarded and the furnace runs on the gateway's stale value.

title: Validate and sanitize inbound Yjs updates server-side to stop malicious clients from injecting oversized or malformed ops
business_context: A collaborative public canvas (anyone with a link can join) trusts client Yjs updates blindly. A malicious user crafted updates that balloon the Y.Doc to gigabytes (zip-bomb-style nested structures) and injected XSS via unsanitized text nodes, taking down rooms and popping alerts for everyone. Abuse reports spiked after the canvas went viral.
constraints: Reject updates that would grow the doc beyond 8MB or 50k nodes
Sanitize text content to strip script/HTML before it reaches other clients
Validate update binary structure before applying (no malformed/oversized frames)
Must not add >5ms latency to legitimate updates
Offending client is rate-limited/banned without disconnecting innocents

before_state.description: Server applies any inbound Yjs update directly and rebroadcasts; no size, structure, or content validation.

issues: No size cap -> doc-bomb DoS
No structure validation -> malformed update can crash applyUpdate
No text sanitization -> stored XSS broadcast to all peers

reasoning: Naive fix: cap raw frame bytes. Insufficient — a small update can still expand the doc enormously (structural amplification) and bytes don't catch XSS. Rejected moving all edits server-authoritative — defeats the CRDT model and adds latency. Chosen: apply the update to a sandbox clone first, enforce node/byte budgets on the resulting state, run a content sanitizer over text deltas, and only commit+broadcast if it passes; offenders hit a token-bucket ban. Cross-layer trace: inbound update -> structural validation (decode without applying) -> apply to a cloned Doc -> measure node count & encoded size delta -> sanitize text via DOMPurify-equivalent -> if OK commit to live doc and broadcast, else drop + penalize sender; tests assert bombs/XSS rejected and latency budget.

after_state.description: Sandbox-validate inbound updates: structure check, node/byte budget on a clone, text sanitization, commit-or-drop, per-sender penalty bucket.

files.purpose: Applies inbound updates to a sandbox clone first and enforces node/byte budgets, committing to the live doc only if within limits — blocking doc-bomb DoS.
Strips script/HTML from collaborative text so stored XSS cannot be broadcast to other clients.
Tracks rejected updates per sender and signals a ban after repeated abuse, without affecting other users.

migration_strategy: Deploy validation in log-only mode behind canvas_guard flag (measure rejects, still apply).
Tune budgets against real legitimate large canvases to avoid false positives.
Enable enforcement on new public rooms; keep trusted/private rooms unguarded.
Ramp to all rooms; add sanitizer and penalty bucket.
Add ban list integration; monitor false-positive rate.
Remove the trust-all apply path.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a canvas Y.Doc near budget. Bomb test: craft an update that would push the clone past 8MB/50k nodes; assert validateAndApply returns ok:false and the live doc is unchanged. XSS test: insert text ''; assert sanitizeDoc strips it and peers receive clean text. Malformed test: feed truncated binary; assert reason 'malformed' and no crash. Latency test: validate a legitimate small update; assert added latency <5ms. Penalty test: 6 bad updates from one user triggers ban while a parallel good user is unaffected. Side-effect: rejected updates are not broadcast.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the trust boundary, applyUpdate executes whatever structure the client sends directly on the shared live Doc, so a crafted update that expands into millions of nested map entries inflates the room's memory for everyone before any check can run — and injected markup is rebroadcast verbatim as stored XSS.

title: Bridge a React Native mobile editor to the same Yjs doc as web using IndexedDB-equivalent offline persistence and resync
business_context: A field-research note app has a web editor on Yjs and a React Native mobile app that uses a separate REST sync, so notes edited on mobile offline don't merge with web edits and overwrite them on upload. Researchers in the field lose annotations made on web by colleagues. 5k researchers, 60% primarily mobile, frequently offline for hours.
constraints: Mobile offline edits must merge with web edits, never overwrite
Local persistence must survive app kill/restart on the device
Resync on reconnect must be incremental (delta), not full doc
Same Y.Doc room shared by web and mobile
Battery: no constant polling; event-driven sync

before_state.description: Mobile keeps notes in AsyncStorage as JSON and uploads the whole note on reconnect, overwriting the server's Yjs-derived state.

issues: Whole-note upload overwrites concurrent web edits
Plain JSON, not Yjs -> can't merge
No incremental delta; full text every sync

reasoning: Naive fix: send mobile text through the same REST endpoint and merge server-side. Server can't merge plain text with a Yjs doc meaningfully. Rejected running a headless web editor in a webview — heavy and battery-hostile. Chosen: run Yjs natively in React Native with a local persistence provider (y-indexeddb equivalent via a SQLite/MMKV-backed provider) and the same y-websocket room as web, so mobile offline edits accumulate as Yjs updates and merge on reconnect. Cross-layer trace: mobile mounts a Y.Doc with an offline provider persisting updates to device storage; edits go through a TextInput bound to Y.Text via a binding; on connectivity (NetInfo event) the websocket provider syncs deltas both ways; web is unchanged; tests assert offline-edit merge with web.

after_state.description: Native Yjs on mobile with device-backed offline persistence and shared y-websocket room; event-driven delta resync; merges with web.

files.purpose: Persists the Y.Doc to fast device storage so offline edits survive app kill and are available to merge on reconnect.
Event-driven (NetInfo) connect/disconnect of the shared y-websocket room so resync is incremental and battery-friendly.
Binds the mobile TextInput to the shared Y.Text so edits become Yjs updates that merge with web rather than overwriting.

migration_strategy: Ship native Yjs path behind mobile_crdt flag; legacy REST sync stays as fallback.
Migrate a note to Yjs on first mobile open; keep REST mirror for rollback.
Canary a research cohort; force offline edits on both web and mobile and verify merge.
Ramp to all; auto-fallback to REST if Yjs provider repeatedly fails on a device.
Stop REST mirror once merge verified across offline scenarios.
Optimize MMKV write frequency and add gc/snapshot for large notes.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a note Y.Doc with shared content. Offline-merge test: web appends a paragraph while mobile (offline) inserts a sentence mid-doc; reconnect mobile; assert both edits present on web and mobile, none overwritten. Persistence test: apply offline edits, simulate app kill (recreate doc with attachOffline), assert edits restored. Delta test: assert resync transmits only new updates. Side-effect: device storage holds the encoded state; NetInfo offline tears down the provider. Chaos test: flap connectivity rapidly; assert no duplicate provider and convergence holds.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the upload boundary, mobile PUTs the entire note text it edited offline, replacing the server value; any paragraph a web colleague added while the researcher was offline was never in the mobile copy, so the PUT erases it with no merge.

title: Replace polling-based 'who is typing' with awareness-driven typing indicators that clear on blur and disconnect
business_context: A customer-support live-chat console shows 'agent is typing' to teammates handling the same ticket. The indicator is driven by a 3s poll of a typing flag in Postgres, so it lags badly and gets stuck on (the flag never clears if the agent navigates away), confusing teams into thinking a colleague is still composing. 600 agents, busy tickets have 3 collaborators.
constraints: Typing indicator must appear within 300ms and clear within 1s of stop
Must clear immediately on tab blur, navigation, or disconnect
No DB writes for transient typing state
Works across multiple console instances with Redis
Dedup multiple tabs of one agent into a single indicator

before_state.description: Typing state is a boolean column polled every 3s; it lags and gets stuck because nothing clears it on navigation/disconnect.

files.content:
export function setTyping(ticketId:string, agentId:string, v:boolean){ api.post('/typing',{ ticketId, agentId, v }); }
setInterval(async()=>{ const r=await api.get('/typing?ticket='+ticketId); render(r.typers); },3000); // 3s poll lag
issues: 3s poll causes visible lag
No clear on navigation/disconnect -> stuck indicator
DB write per keystroke-burst; multi-tab double-counts

reasoning: Naive fix: shorten the poll to 500ms. Hammers the DB and still gets stuck because nothing clears on disconnect. Rejected a bespoke typing WS message with manual timeouts — reinvents awareness TTL. Chosen: Yjs awareness for transient typing state (no DB), with a local debounce to set typing=true on input and a 1s idle timeout to clear, plus visibilitychange/pagehide to clear immediately, deduped by agentId; awareness TTL handles disconnects automatically. Cross-layer trace: input handler sets awareness typing field (debounced), idle timer clears it; blur/pagehide clear; relay fans awareness across instances via Redis; UI groups typers by agentId; tests assert appear/clear timings and stuck-state elimination.

after_state.description: Awareness-driven typing state: debounced set, 1s idle clear, immediate clear on blur/pagehide, agentId dedup, Redis fanout, no DB writes.

files.purpose: Sets transient typing in awareness on input and clears it after 1s idle or immediately on tab-hide/navigation, with no DB writes.
Renders typing teammates deduped by agentId, excluding self, reacting instantly to awareness changes.
Fans typing awareness across console instances via Redis so teammates on any instance see the indicator.

migration_strategy: Add awareness typing behind typing_v2 flag; legacy poll stays.
Canary internal support team; verify <300ms appear and clear-on-navigate.
Ramp to all; remove the typing DB column writes.
Add Redis fanout for multi-instance.
Delete the polling code and endpoint.
Tune idle timeout per locale typing speed.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a ticket Awareness with 2 agents. Appear test: agent A fires input; assert B sees indicator within 300ms (awareness change). Clear-idle test: stop input, advance 1s fake timer; assert indicator clears. Clear-navigate test: dispatch pagehide for A; assert B's indicator clears immediately. Dedup test: A in two tabs typing; assert one indicator. Side-effect: zero DB writes during typing. Chaos test: A disconnects ungracefully; assert awareness TTL clears the indicator without a stuck flag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the navigation boundary, the agent leaves the page without an explicit clear call, so the Postgres typing flag stays true and every 3s poll keeps rendering 'agent is typing' for a teammate who left minutes ago.

title: Reconcile optimistic local CRDT edits with server-rejected ops to prevent permanent UI/server divergence
business_context: A collaborative budgeting app applies CRDT edits optimistically, but the server enforces a business rule (a category total can't exceed an approved cap) and silently drops violating ops. The client already applied them locally, so the user's screen permanently shows numbers the server rejected, leading to budget overruns submitted to finance. ~50 budgets/day diverge.
constraints: Server rule violations must roll back the local optimistic op, not leave it applied
Other valid concurrent edits must remain
User must get clear feedback on why an edit was rejected
Rollback must not flicker unrelated parts of the doc
Works with Yjs as the transport CRDT

before_state.description: Client applies CRDT op locally and sends it; server validates and drops violations without telling the client, so the client keeps the rejected state forever.

files.content:
import * as Y from 'yjs';
export function setAmount(ydoc:Y.Doc, cat:string, amount:number){ ydoc.getMap('amounts').set(cat,amount); /* sent via provider */ }
issues: No server ack/nack channel for rule rejections
Local op stays applied even when server drops it -> permanent divergence
No user feedback on rejection

files.content: export function onUpdate(update:any){ if(violatesCap(update)) return; /* silently drop */ apply(update); broadcast(update); }
issues: Silent drop with no nack
Server and client diverge with no reconciliation path

reasoning: Naive fix: validate on the client before applying. Insufficient — the cap depends on concurrent edits the client hasn't seen yet, so a locally-valid op can be invalid post-merge. Rejected making all edits server-round-trip (pessimistic) — kills the responsive feel CRDTs provide. Chosen: keep optimistic apply but add an explicit accept/reject protocol: each op carries a client op-id; the server validates against converged state and emits an ack or a reject(opId, reason); on reject the client applies a compensating inverse via a scoped UndoManager and shows feedback, while keeping all accepted ops. Cross-layer trace: client tracks pending ops by id in a local origin; server validates on converged state and nacks with reason; client, on nack, undoes exactly that op via the UndoManager's stack item and surfaces a toast; tests assert rejected op rolled back and others retained.

after_state.description: Optimistic CRDT with explicit ack/reject by op-id; rejected ops compensated via scoped inverse; user feedback; accepted concurrent ops retained.

files.purpose: Applies edits optimistically with a tracked op-id and rolls back exactly the rejected op via the UndoManager while keeping accepted ones.
Validates each op against converged state on a clone, then explicitly acks or rejects with a reason instead of silently dropping.
Routes server ack/reject messages to the optimistic editor so the client reconciles instead of diverging.

migration_strategy: Add op-id + ack/reject protocol behind reconcile_v2 flag; legacy silent-drop stays.
Canary: verify rejected ops roll back and accepted concurrent ops persist.
Ramp to all budgets; show rejection toasts; monitor divergence metric (client hash vs server) -> 0.
Add reason codes for each rule.
Remove silent-drop path.
Add a divergence watchdog that forces resync if a hash mismatch slips through.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a budget at cap 1000 with categories summing 900. Reject test: client sets a category +200 (would be 1100); server rejects; assert client rolls back to 900 via undo and shows the reason, server unchanged. Retain test: a concurrent valid edit (+50) by another user is acked and remains after the rollback. Side-effect: pending map empties on ack/reject; no orphaned op-id. Hash test: after reconciliation, client doc hash == server doc hash. Chaos test: ack and reject arrive out of order / duplicated; assert idempotent handling and no double-undo.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the validation boundary, the server drops the cap-violating update without telling the client, but the client already applied it optimistically and has no signal to undo, so the user's screen keeps showing the over-cap total that the server will never store — and that wrong number gets submitted to finance.

title: Fix collaborative comment threads losing replies under concurrency by modeling threads as a Yjs nested map with stable ids
business_context: A design-review tool pins comment threads on artboards. Replies are stored as an array PATCHed wholesale; when two reviewers reply to the same thread simultaneously, one reply vanishes. On high-traffic launch reviews (40 reviewers) ~10% of threads lose a reply, so feedback is silently dropped and design decisions are made on incomplete input.
constraints: Concurrent replies to one thread must all persist
Reply ordering by timestamp must be stable and identical across clients
Resolving a thread is a concurrent-safe toggle
Mentions must trigger notifications exactly once per reply
Existing threads as JSON arrays must migrate

before_state.description: Replies are a JS array; adding a reply PATCHes the whole array, so concurrent adds clobber. Resolve is a boolean LWW.

files.content:
export function addReply(thread:any, reply:any){ thread.replies.push(reply); api.patch('/threads/'+thread.id,{ replies: thread.replies }); }
export function resolve(thread:any){ thread.resolved=true; api.patch('/threads/'+thread.id,{ resolved:true }); }
issues: Whole-array PATCH clobbers concurrent replies
Boolean resolve is LWW; un-resolve races
Notifications fire from client -> duplicates on retry

reasoning: Naive fix: append-only server endpoint POST /replies. Solves reply loss but resolve toggle still races and notifications can duplicate on client retries. Rejected a relational replies table with its own realtime channel — splits the doc across two sync systems and complicates ordering. Chosen: model each thread as a Y.Map containing a Y.Map of replies keyed by reply-id (concurrent-safe inserts), a resolved flag as a CRDT field, and emit mention notifications from a server-side observer that dedupes by reply-id. Cross-layer trace: UI inserts a reply Y.Map keyed by uuid; clients render sorted by timestamp; resolve is a Y.Map boolean merged commutatively; a server Y.Doc observer detects new reply ids and enqueues notifications exactly once; legacy arrays import; tests assert no lost replies and single notification.

after_state.description: Threads as Y.Map with reply sub-map keyed by uuid (concurrent-safe), CRDT resolved flag, server observer dedupes mention notifications by reply-id.

files.purpose: Stores replies in a uuid-keyed Y.Map so concurrent replies never collide, and renders them in stable timestamp order.
Fires mention notifications server-side keyed by reply-id with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING, guaranteeing exactly-once delivery even on retries.

files.content:
import * as Y from 'yjs';
export function toggleResolved(ydoc:Y.Doc, threadId:string, v:boolean){ (ydoc.getMap('threads').get(threadId) as Y.Map<any>).set('resolved', v); }
files.purpose: Resolves via a CRDT map field so concurrent resolve/un-resolve converge deterministically.

migration_strategy: Add Y.Doc thread store behind threads_crdt flag; import legacy arrays to keyed maps; dual-write JSON for rollback.
Move notifications to the server observer with the dedupe table; stop client-side notify.
Canary a review; verify no lost replies and single notifications.
Ramp to all reviews; auto-fallback to JSON if observer errors.
Stop dual-writing JSON after a clean window.
Add ordering tie-break (ts then id) for identical timestamps.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a thread with 2 replies. Concurrency test: two reviewers add replies on two peers simultaneously; merge; assert both replies present on both, sorted by ts. Notification test: a reply with mentions inserted twice (retry) yields exactly one notifications row and one enqueue. Resolve test: concurrent resolve(true)/resolve(false) converge to one deterministic value on all peers. Side-effect: notifications.reply_id unique. Migration test: legacy array imports to keyed map preserving order. Chaos test: replay a reply add out of order/duplicated; assert idempotent (same uuid key) with no duplicate reply.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the concurrent-reply boundary, each reviewer pushes onto their local array and PATCHes the whole replies list; the second PATCH overwrites the first, so the reply added by the first reviewer — absent from the second's array — disappears and that feedback is lost from the review.

title: Migrate a homegrown OT text server to Yjs without downtime using a dual-write bridge and convergence verification
business_context: A legacy docs product runs a custom operational-transform server that's buggy (rare but catastrophic divergence) and unmaintained. The team wants Yjs but can't take downtime for 900k active documents or risk a flag-day cutover. Past big-bang migrations corrupted docs. Need a gradual, verifiable migration with instant rollback.
constraints: Zero downtime; users keep editing during migration
No document may diverge between OT and Yjs during the bridge phase
Instant per-doc rollback to OT if convergence check fails
Migration runs while docs are actively edited
Verifiable: prove OT and Yjs render identical text before cutover

before_state.description: All edits flow through the custom OT server. Yjs not present. No bridge or verification.

files.content: export function applyOp(docId:string, op:Op){ const doc=load(docId); const xformed=transformAgainstHistory(doc,op); doc.apply(xformed); persist(docId,doc); broadcast(docId,xformed); }
issues: Buggy transform causes rare divergence
Unmaintained; no path to Yjs
No way to verify equivalence with a new engine

reasoning: Naive fix: convert all docs to Yjs in a batch job and cut over. This is the exact flag-day that corrupted docs before — any in-flight OT op during conversion is lost. Rejected running Yjs as the sole engine immediately — no way to verify correctness against live edits first. Chosen: a bridge that, per doc, applies each incoming OT op to BOTH the OT engine and a shadow Yjs doc (translating OT ops to Yjs ops), continuously compares rendered text, and only flips a doc's authoritative engine to Yjs once N consecutive convergence checks pass; rollback flips the flag back. Cross-layer trace: edits hit the OT server which also forwards a translated op to a Yjs bridge; a verifier compares OT text vs Yjs text per op and records mismatches; a per-doc engine flag (ot|bridge|yjs) controls which engine is authoritative for broadcast; tests assert no divergence and safe cutover.

after_state.description: Dual-write bridge translating OT ops to Yjs, continuous convergence verification, per-doc engine flag with instant rollback, gradual cutover.

files.purpose: Translates each legacy OT op into the equivalent Y.Text op so the shadow Yjs doc tracks the OT doc exactly.
Compares OT and Yjs rendered text after every op, logging mismatches and tracking a convergence streak that gates cutover.
Routes each doc's authoritative engine via a flag, auto-promoting to Yjs after 200 clean checks and supporting instant per-doc rollback.

migration_strategy: Deploy the bridge: OT remains authoritative; every op also applies to shadow Yjs and runs the verifier (bridge phase).
Run bridge across all active docs; review migration_mismatch table; fix translation bugs until mismatches stop.
Promote docs to engine='yjs' automatically after 200 consecutive clean checks; Yjs becomes authoritative for those.
Move clients of promoted docs onto the y-websocket provider; OT path kept warm.
Any post-cutover divergence triggers rollback() to OT and re-enters bridge.
Decommission the OT server only after 100% promoted and a full clean audit window.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a doc with a known op stream. Equivalence test: feed 1,000 OT ops (inserts/deletes at random positions) through the bridge; assert OT text == Yjs text after each op (verifier returns true, streak grows). Cutover test: reach streak 200; assert engine flips to yjs and subsequent edits use Y.Text. Mismatch test: inject a deliberately mistranslated op; assert a migration_mismatch row, streak reset to 0, no promotion. Rollback test: call rollback(); assert engine back to ot and clients reattach to OT. Chaos test: concurrent ops during promotion; assert no op lost across the engine flip and final texts match.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the cutover boundary, a batch conversion snapshots each doc and rebuilds it in Yjs, but any OT op a user submits during that snapshot window is applied to the about-to-be-discarded OT doc and never translated, so it's silently lost the instant the doc flips to Yjs — exactly the corruption that sank previous flag-day attempts.

title: Build a Python FastAPI Yjs sync backend (ypy) to replace a Node relay so the data team can run CRDT docs in their stack
business_context: A data-science notebook product is Python-first (FastAPI). They prototyped collaboration with a separate Node y-websocket service, but operating a second runtime broke their deploy/observability and the Node relay didn't share auth with FastAPI. They need CRDT sync inside FastAPI. 3k data scientists co-edit notebooks, 2-4 per notebook.
constraints: Single Python runtime; no separate Node service
Reuse FastAPI's existing JWT auth dependency
Handle Yjs binary protocol (sync + awareness) correctly from Python
Persist notebook Y.Doc to Postgres via asyncpg
Scale across uvicorn workers with Redis pub/sub

before_state.description: A standalone Node y-websocket relay runs beside FastAPI with its own (missing) auth; two runtimes, split observability.

files.content:
const { setupWSConnection }=require('y-websocket/bin/utils');
require('ws').Server({ port:1234 }).on('connection',(ws,req)=> setupWSConnection(ws,req)); // separate runtime, no FastAPI auth
issues: Second runtime to deploy/monitor
No shared auth with FastAPI -> open relay
No Postgres persistence integrated with the app DB

reasoning: Naive fix: keep Node but add a token check there. Still two runtimes and duplicated auth logic that drifts. Rejected reimplementing CRDT from scratch in Python — months of work and a divergence risk. Chosen: use ypy (Python Yjs bindings) inside FastAPI WebSocket routes, implement the Yjs sync protocol (messageSync step1/step2/update + awareness) in Python, reuse the FastAPI JWT dependency, persist via asyncpg, and fan out across uvicorn workers with Redis. Cross-layer trace: FastAPI WS route authenticates via the same JWT dependency; handles the binary Yjs protocol with ypy YDoc; applies/encodes updates; persists on debounce to Postgres; publishes updates to Redis so other workers broadcast; tests assert protocol correctness and auth.

after_state.description: FastAPI WebSocket Yjs sync using ypy, shared JWT auth, asyncpg persistence, Redis fanout across uvicorn workers. One runtime.

files.content:
import y_py as Y
from struct import pack
MSG_SYNC=0; MSG_AWARENESS=1
SYNC_STEP1=0; SYNC_STEP2=1; SYNC_UPDATE=2

def encode_sync_step1(doc:Y.YDoc)->bytes:
    sv=Y.encode_state_vector(doc)
    return bytes([MSG_SYNC,SYNC_STEP1])+sv

def encode_sync_step2(doc:Y.YDoc, sv:bytes)->bytes:
    diff=Y.encode_state_as_update(doc, sv)
    return bytes([MSG_SYNC,SYNC_STEP2])+diff

def apply_update(doc:Y.YDoc, update:bytes):
    Y.apply_update(doc, update)
files.purpose: Implements the Yjs binary sync protocol in Python with ypy so the FastAPI server speaks exactly what y-websocket clients expect.

files.content:
from fastapi import APIRouter, WebSocket, Depends
import y_py as Y
from .protocol import MSG_SYNC, SYNC_STEP1, SYNC_STEP2, SYNC_UPDATE, encode_sync_step2, apply_update
from .auth import jwt_ws
from .store import load_doc, persist, publish, subscribe
router=APIRouter()
rooms:dict[str,Y.YDoc]={}

@router.websocket('/collab/{notebook_id}')
async def collab(ws:WebSocket, notebook_id:str, user=Depends(jwt_ws)):
    await ws.accept()
    doc=rooms.get(notebook_id) or await load_doc(notebook_id); rooms[notebook_id]=doc
    await ws.send_bytes(encode_sync_step2(doc, b''))  # initial state
    async def on_remote(update:bytes):
        apply_update(doc, update); await ws.send_bytes(bytes([MSG_SYNC,SYNC_UPDATE])+update)
    unsub=await subscribe(notebook_id, on_remote)
    try:
        while True:
            data=await ws.receive_bytes()
            if data[0]==MSG_SYNC and data[1]==SYNC_UPDATE:
                apply_update(doc, data[2:]); await persist(notebook_id, doc); await publish(notebook_id, data[2:])
    finally:
        await unsub()
files.purpose: FastAPI WebSocket route handling Yjs sync with shared JWT auth, persisting and fanning out updates.

files.content:
import y_py as Y, asyncpg, base64
from redis.asyncio import Redis
redis=Redis()
async def load_doc(nid:str)->Y.YDoc:
    doc=Y.YDoc()
    row=await pool.fetchrow('SELECT state FROM notebook_doc WHERE id=$1', nid)
    if row and row['state']: Y.apply_update(doc, row['state'])
    return doc
async def persist(nid:str, doc:Y.YDoc):
    state=Y.encode_state_as_update(doc)
    await pool.execute('INSERT INTO notebook_doc(id,state) VALUES($1,$2) ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET state=$2', nid, state)
async def publish(nid:str, update:bytes): await redis.publish(f'nb:{nid}', base64.b64encode(update))
async def subscribe(nid:str, cb):
    ps=redis.pubsub(); await ps.subscribe(f'nb:{nid}')
    async def reader():
        async for m in ps.listen():
            if m['type']=='message': await cb(base64.b64decode(m['data']))
    import asyncio; task=asyncio.create_task(reader())
    async def stop(): task.cancel(); await ps.unsubscribe(f'nb:{nid}')
    return stop
files.purpose: Persists the ypy doc to Postgres via asyncpg and fans updates across uvicorn workers with Redis pub/sub.

migration_strategy: Stand up the FastAPI ypy route behind py_collab flag; keep the Node relay running.
Point internal data-team clients at the FastAPI endpoint; verify byte-level protocol parity with y-websocket.
Canary 10% of notebooks to FastAPI; compare convergence and latency vs Node.
Ramp to 100%; clients switch WS URL via config.
Decommission the Node relay after a clean week.
Add Redis fanout and persistence tuning under load.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a notebook ypy doc. Protocol test: a real y-websocket JS client connects to the FastAPI route, edits; assert the Python doc applies the update and the client converges (byte-compatible sync). Auth test: connection without a valid JWT is rejected by the dependency. Persistence test: edit, persist, reload via load_doc; assert state matches. Fanout test: two uvicorn workers sharing Redis converge a doc. Side-effect: notebook_doc.state updated. Chaos test: Redis pubsub drop and recovery; assert updates resume and no doc divergence.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the runtime boundary, keeping the Node relay means auth, deploy, and observability live in a second stack the Python team can't instrument; the relay's JWT check drifts from FastAPI's, leaving an open sync endpoint whenever the two auth implementations disagree.

title: Fix avatar-stack overflow and flicker on large rooms by capping rendered cursors and aggregating the rest
business_context: A collaborative slide-deck tool renders a live cursor and avatar for every participant. On all-hands decks with 300 viewers, rendering 300 absolutely-positioned cursor DOM nodes tanks frame rate to ~8fps and the avatar bar overflows the toolbar. Presenters can't navigate the deck smoothly during company meetings.
constraints: Render at 60fps with up to 300 participants
Show at most 8 individual cursors (nearest/most-active), aggregate the rest as a count
Avatar bar shows top 5 + '+N more'
Cursor DOM must be virtualized/reused, not recreated each frame
Presenter cursor always rendered

before_state.description: One DOM node per participant cursor, recreated every awareness change; avatar bar renders all participants. O(N) DOM churn per frame.

issues: One DOM node per participant -> 300 nodes
Recreated each render -> layout thrash, flicker
Avatar bar overflows; no aggregation

reasoning: Naive fix: throttle awareness updates. Reduces update rate but you still mount 300 DOM nodes. Rejected canvas-rendering all 300 cursors — smooth but loses accessible name labels and hit-testing for hover. Chosen: cap individually-rendered cursors to the 8 most relevant (presenter + nearest to viewport center or most recently active), aggregate the rest into a single 'N others' heat-count, and virtualize cursor DOM with a fixed pool reused across frames. Cross-layer trace: awareness provides all states; a selector ranks and slices top-8 (presenter pinned); a fixed pool of 8 cursor nodes is repositioned (transform) instead of remounted; avatar bar shows top-5 + overflow; tests assert node count cap and fps.

after_state.description: Top-8 cursor cap (presenter pinned) with the rest aggregated; fixed reused DOM pool repositioned via transform; avatar bar top-5 + '+N'.

files.purpose: Ranks cursors to render only the 8 most relevant (presenter pinned, then most-active/nearest), aggregating the remainder into a count.
Reuses a fixed pool of 8 cursor nodes, repositioning with CSS transform (no remount) to hold 60fps, and shows an aggregate for the rest.
Caps the avatar bar to 5 avatars plus an overflow count so the toolbar never overflows.

migration_strategy: Add capped renderer behind deck_cursor_cap flag; legacy all-cursors path stays.
Canary all-hands decks; measure fps with 100+ participants.
Ramp to all decks; tune the cap and ranking weights.
Add aggregate heat indicator and presenter pinning.
Remove the per-participant DOM renderer.
A/B the limit (8 vs 12) for usability.
testing_strategy: Fixture: awareness with 300 states, one presenter. Cap test: assert selectTop returns exactly 8 (presenter included) plus othersCount=292. Reuse test: re-render with moved cursors; assert the same 8 DOM nodes are reused (refs stable), only transforms change. fps test (perf harness): 300 cursors updating; assert render commits stay within frame budget. Avatar test: assert 5 avatars + '+295'. Side-effect: presenter always in the top set even if idle. Chaos test: rapid join/leave churn; assert no node count growth beyond 8 and no flicker (no remount).
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the 300-participant boundary, mapping every awareness state to a fresh DOM node remounts 300 absolutely-positioned elements on each cursor change, forcing full layout/paint per frame and collapsing the deck to single-digit fps while the avatar bar overflows the toolbar.

title: Stop nested-list reordering corruption in a collaborative outliner by using Yjs Y.Array tree with move via delete+insert transaction
business_context: A knowledge-base outliner (nested bullets) lets teams co-edit hierarchical outlines. Moving a bullet (with children) under another while a teammate edits the destination corrupts the tree: children orphan or duplicate. The current model stores a flat list with parent ids and integer order, PATCHed per node. ~8% of concurrent outline edits corrupt structure on big docs (2k nodes).
constraints: Moving a subtree must keep all descendants attached, concurrently safe
No cycles may form (can't move a node under its own descendant)
Sibling order stable and identical across clients
Collapse/expand state is per-user, not shared
Existing flat parent/order rows migrate

before_state.description: Flat nodes with parent_id and int order, PATCHed individually. Concurrent moves race and an interleaved destination edit orphans or duplicates children.

files.content: export function moveNode(id:string, newParent:string, order:number){ api.patch('/nodes/'+id,{ parent_id:newParent, order }); /* children unchanged, may orphan */ }
issues: Per-node PATCH races; children left pointing at moved parent inconsistently
Integer order collides on concurrent inserts
No cycle prevention

reasoning: Naive fix: move the subtree in one server transaction. Removes orphaning but still LWW on concurrent destination edits and integer order collides. Rejected a nested-set (lft/rgt) model — elegant for reads but every concurrent move rewrites huge ranges, terrible under collaboration. Chosen: represent the tree as a Y.Map of node Y.Maps where each node holds a Y.Array of child ids ordered by fractional index; a move is one Y transaction that deletes the id from the old parent's child array and inserts into the new parent's, with a cycle check; collapse state lives in awareness/local. Cross-layer trace: UI computes target fractional key and runs a transaction (cycle-guarded) moving the id; children follow because they're referenced by id, not position; provider syncs; collapse stored locally; legacy rows import; tests assert subtree integrity and no cycles.

after_state.description: Tree as Y.Map of nodes each with a fractional-ordered Y.Array of child ids; atomic cycle-guarded move transaction; per-user collapse state; legacy import.

files.purpose: Moves a subtree atomically by id (children follow automatically) with fractional ordering and a cycle guard.
Keeps collapse/expand state local per user so one person collapsing doesn't collapse everyone's outline.
Converts legacy flat parent/order rows into the Y.Map tree with fractional ordering preserving structure.

migration_strategy: Add Y.Doc outline store behind outline_crdt flag; import flat rows on first open; dual-write flat rows for rollback.
Canary internal KB; run concurrent subtree moves and verify no orphan/duplicate.
Ramp to all; auto-rollback a doc to flat model if a tree-integrity check fails.
Enforce cycle guard server-side too.
Stop dual-writing flat rows after a clean window.
Move collapse state fully to local storage.
testing_strategy: Fixture: an outline with A>(B>(D),C). Concurrent move test: peer1 moves B (with D) under C while peer2 edits C's text; merge; assert D still under B, B under C, no duplicate, identical order both peers. Cycle test: attempt to move A under D; assert it throws and tree unchanged. Order test: 50 random moves in two orders converge identically. Collapse test: peer1 collapses B; assert peer2's outline unaffected. Migration test: flat rows import to identical tree. Chaos test: replay a move out of order/duplicated; assert subtree integrity and no resurrection of a deleted node.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the concurrent subtree-move boundary, moving a node by PATCHing only its parent_id leaves its children referencing the moved node while a concurrent destination edit reorders siblings; with integer order and no atomic subtree operation the children either point into a stale ordering (orphan) or get reattached twice (duplicate).

title: Prevent reconnect duplicate-application storms by deduplicating Yjs sync with state vectors and idempotent message handling
business_context: A collaborative form-filling app (insurance claims) sees clients flapping on poor connections. On each reconnect the client re-sends its full update history and the server re-broadcasts everything, so peers re-apply thousands of already-seen updates, spiking CPU and briefly showing stale intermediate states. Agents on 3G report multi-second freezes per reconnect.
constraints: Reconnect must sync only the delta the peer is missing, not full history
Re-applying an already-seen update must be a no-op (idempotent)
No stale intermediate render during catch-up
Works with lossy connections that reconnect frequently
Bounded CPU on reconnect even for large docs (10k ops)

before_state.description: On reconnect the client sends its whole encoded state and the server rebroadcasts all updates; peers re-apply everything.

files.content:
export function onReconnect(doc, ws){ ws.send(encodeStateAsUpdate(doc)); /* whole history */ }
export function onMessage(doc, data){ applyUpdate(doc, data); rerender(); /* re-applies seen updates, re-render each */ }
issues: Sends whole state every reconnect -> O(doc) traffic and CPU
Re-applies already-seen updates -> redundant work and stale flashes
Re-render per applied update during catch-up

reasoning: Naive fix: track a 'last sync id' and only send after it. Brittle across multi-peer topologies and lossy reorder. Rejected diffing rendered DOM to suppress flashes — treats the symptom. Chosen: use the Yjs sync protocol properly — exchange state vectors (sync step1) so each side computes the minimal missing diff (step2), apply updates which are inherently idempotent in Yjs, and batch a single re-render after catch-up completes. Cross-layer trace: on reconnect the client sends its state vector; server replies with only the missing update; applyUpdate is idempotent (Yjs ignores already-integrated structs); a 'synced' event triggers one render; tests assert delta-only traffic, idempotent re-apply, single render.

after_state.description: State-vector-based delta sync (sync step1/step2), idempotent applyUpdate, single batched render after catch-up.

files.purpose: Exchanges state vectors so each reconnect transfers only the minimal missing diff instead of full history.
Applies updates (idempotently) and renders once after a catch-up batch, eliminating per-update stale flashes and redundant work.
Routes the sync protocol and batches catch-up renders to one per frame.

migration_strategy: Add state-vector sync behind sync_v2 flag; legacy whole-state reconnect stays.
Canary 3G/lossy cohort; measure reconnect traffic and CPU.
Ramp to all; verify no stale-flash and single render per catch-up.
Remove whole-state reconnect path.
Add metrics on reconnect delta size.
Tune flush interval for very large docs.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a doc with 10k ops; a peer missing the last 5. Delta test: on reconnect assert the server sends only those 5 ops (state-vector diff), not all 10k. Idempotency test: apply the same update twice; assert the second apply changes nothing (doc hash stable) and only one render. Traffic test: assert reconnect bytes proportional to missing ops, not doc size. Side-effect: render called once per catch-up batch. Chaos test: flap reconnect 10x rapidly with reordered/duplicated updates; assert convergence, bounded CPU, no stale intermediate render visible.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the reconnect boundary, sending the whole encoded state and re-broadcasting all updates means every peer re-applies thousands of structs it already integrated and re-renders per update, so a flaky 3G client triggers an O(doc) CPU storm and a flash of intermediate states on every reconnect.

title: Make a collaborative map-annotation tool converge geo edits with a CRDT over geohash-keyed features and presence
business_context: A logistics ops tool lets dispatchers co-annotate a live map (route polylines, no-go zones, pins). Concurrent edits to the same feature (dragging a pin while another moves the same route) overwrite each other via REST PUT, so dispatchers act on a stale map. During a storm reroute, two dispatchers' edits collided and a truck was sent into a flooded zone. 200 dispatchers, peak 15 on one region map.
constraints: Concurrent geometry edits to different features must never block
Same-feature concurrent edits must converge deterministically
Feature lookups by viewport (geohash) must stay fast at 50k features
Live dispatcher cursors shown as map markers
Existing GeoJSON features must migrate

before_state.description: Features stored as GeoJSON rows, edited via PUT of the whole feature; concurrent edits overwrite; no live presence.

files.content: export function updateFeature(f:GeoJSON.Feature){ api.put('/features/'+f.id,{ feature:f }); } // whole-feature LWW
issues: Whole-feature PUT overwrites concurrent edits
No live presence on the map
No viewport-efficient sync; pulls all features

reasoning: Naive fix: field-level merge of geometry on the server. Geometry (coordinate arrays) doesn't field-merge meaningfully and concurrent vertex drags still clobber. Rejected per-vertex OT — overkill and fragile for polylines. Chosen: a Yjs doc where features are a Y.Map keyed by feature id, each feature a Y.Map holding properties and a Y.Array of coordinates (vertex-level CRDT), plus a geohash index in the Y.Doc for viewport sync, and dispatcher cursors via awareness. Concurrent edits to different features are independent; same-feature vertex edits merge at vertex granularity; whole-feature replace avoided. Cross-layer trace: UI edits vertices in the feature's Y.Array; viewport subscribes by geohash prefix; awareness carries dispatcher position; legacy GeoJSON imports; tests assert vertex-level convergence and viewport correctness.

after_state.description: Yjs feature map with vertex-level Y.Array coordinates, geohash index for viewport sync, dispatcher cursors via awareness; legacy GeoJSON imported.

files.purpose: Edits geometry at vertex granularity in a CRDT and maintains a geohash index so concurrent feature edits converge and viewport queries stay fast.
Shows each dispatcher's live position as a map marker via awareness, separate from the persistent feature CRDT.
Imports legacy GeoJSON into the Yjs feature map with a geohash index, preserving geometry.

migration_strategy: Add Yjs map doc behind map_crdt flag per region; import GeoJSON on first open; dual-write GeoJSON for rollback.
Canary one region; run concurrent feature edits and verify convergence + viewport correctness.
Ramp region by region; auto-rollback a region to GeoJSON if a geometry-integrity check fails.
Add dispatcher awareness markers.
Stop dual-writing GeoJSON after a clean window.
Tune geohash precision per zoom level.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a region with a polyline feature P and a pin F. Concurrency test: dispatcher A drags vertex 2 of P while B moves F; merge; assert both edits applied independently on all peers. Same-feature test: A and B drag different vertices of P concurrently; assert both vertex moves converge. Viewport test: featuresInViewport returns only features whose geohash matches the prefix. Presence test: dispatcher markers reflect live positions and clear on disconnect. Migration test: GeoJSON imports to identical geometry. Chaos test: replay a vertex move out of order; assert deterministic final geometry and correct geohash index.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the same-feature boundary, two dispatchers PUT the whole feature after dragging different vertices; the second PUT writes its full coordinate array over the first, discarding the other dispatcher's vertex move, so the rendered route reflects only one edit and the truck is routed on a stale path.

title: Eliminate Yjs awareness memory leak from never-removed states by cleaning up on unmount and stale-client GC
business_context: A long-running collaborative dashboard accumulates awareness states from clients that navigated away without proper teardown. Over a day the awareness map on a busy room holds thousands of dead client entries, leaking memory and rendering hundreds of phantom cursors. Browser tabs left open for hours slow to a crawl. The ops dashboard is meant to run 24/7 on wall displays.
constraints: Awareness states for departed clients must be removed within 30s
Component unmount must fully tear down provider and awareness
Wall-display tabs open 24h must not grow memory unbounded
Must not remove states of still-connected clients
Works behind the standard y-websocket provider

before_state.description: Provider created in a component but never destroyed on unmount; awareness states from gone clients linger; no stale GC.

issues: No cleanup on unmount -> providers and awareness leak
Stale client states never garbage-collected
Phantom cursors accumulate on wall displays

reasoning: Naive fix: call provider.destroy() on unmount. Necessary but insufficient — a client that crashes or navigates without unmounting still leaves a state that the awareness protocol only times out if outline updates flow; on an idle wall display nothing prunes them. Rejected forcing a full page reload nightly — disruptive for 24/7 displays. Chosen: proper useEffect cleanup that destroys provider/doc and removes the local awareness state, plus a periodic stale-state GC that removes awareness entries whose last update exceeds the outline timeout, and a heartbeat so live clients refresh their timestamp. Cross-layer trace: hook returns a cleanup that calls awareness.setLocalState(null) then provider.destroy(); a GC interval removes states older than timeout using awareness meta clocks; a heartbeat updates a benign awareness field to keep live clients fresh; tests assert no leak and live clients retained.

after_state.description: useEffect cleanup destroys provider/doc and clears local state; periodic stale-state GC by awareness clock; heartbeat keeps live clients fresh.

files.purpose: Tears down the provider, doc, and local awareness state on unmount so navigating away doesn't leak.
Removes awareness states whose last update exceeds the timeout, garbage-collecting phantom clients without touching live ones.

files.content:
import { Awareness } from 'y-protocols/awareness';
export function startHeartbeat(awareness:Awareness){ const id=setInterval(()=>{ awareness.setLocalStateField('_hb', Date.now()); }, 10000); return ()=>clearInterval(id); }
files.purpose: Periodically refreshes the local client's awareness timestamp so the GC never evicts a still-connected wall display.

migration_strategy: Add cleanup + GC behind awareness_gc flag; legacy hook stays.
Canary wall-display rooms; monitor awareness state count over 24h.
Ramp to all; verify live clients retained and phantoms pruned within 30s.
Add heartbeat for idle displays.
Remove the leaking hook.
Tune GC interval and timeout for very large rooms.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a room awareness with 5 clients. Leak test: simulate a client departing without setLocalState(null); advance fake clock 31s; run GC; assert that client removed and the other 4 retained. Unmount test: render+unmount the hook; assert provider.destroy called and local state cleared. Heartbeat test: an idle live client's timestamp refreshes every 10s; assert GC never evicts it over a simulated hour. Side-effect: awareness state count stays bounded over a simulated 24h churn. Chaos test: rapid mount/unmount cycles; assert no orphaned providers (count returns to baseline).
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the idle-display boundary, destroying the provider on unmount doesn't help a client that crashed or a peer that left, because on a quiet wall-display room no document updates flow to trigger the awareness protocol's own timeout, so dead entries and their phantom cursors accumulate until the tab grinds to a halt.

title: Add server-side access control to per-field CRDT edits so viewers can edit comments but not the locked contract body
business_context: A legal-contract collaboration tool uses one Yjs doc per contract containing both an editable comment layer and a locked legal body. Because Yjs accepts any update to any part of the doc, a viewer-role user can edit the legally-binding body, not just comments. Compliance flagged that role permissions are advisory only (UI-hidden) and trivially bypassed. 5k contracts, viewer/editor/legal roles.
constraints: Viewers may edit only the 'comments' subtree, never the 'body'
Editors may edit body+comments; legal role may also change lock state
Enforcement must be server-side (UI hiding is insufficient)
Rejected partial updates must not corrupt the shared doc
Permission changes take effect within 30s without reconnect

before_state.description: Single Yjs doc; server applies any client update wholesale; roles enforced only by hiding UI controls.

files.content:
import * as Y from 'yjs';
export function onUpdate(doc:Y.Doc, update:Uint8Array, broadcast:(u:Uint8Array)=>void){ Y.applyUpdate(doc, update); broadcast(update); /* no per-subtree authz */ }
issues: No subtree-level authorization; viewers can edit body
UI-only role hiding is bypassable
No rejection path for unauthorized partial updates

reasoning: Naive fix: split into two docs (body vs comments) with separate auth. Cleaner but breaks single-doc features (cross-references, unified history) and doubles sync plumbing. Rejected client-side enforcement — the whole point is it's bypassable. Chosen: keep one doc but validate each inbound update server-side by applying it to a clone and diffing which subtrees changed; reject (drop + nack) if the user's role isn't permitted to touch a changed subtree; only commit authorized updates. Permissions read from a cache refreshed within 30s. Cross-layer trace: inbound update -> apply to clone -> compute changed top-level keys -> check role policy (viewer: comments only) -> commit+broadcast or reject with reason -> client rolls back via the reconciliation protocol; tests assert viewers blocked from body.

after_state.description: Single doc with server-side per-subtree authorization: clone-diff changed subtrees, enforce role policy, commit-or-reject, fresh permission cache.

files.purpose: Determines which top-level subtrees an update touches and enforces the role policy server-side, blocking viewers from the body.
Commits only authorized updates and nacks unauthorized ones so the shared doc is never corrupted by a forbidden edit.
Resolves roles with a 30s TTL cache so permission changes take effect within 30s without a reconnect.

migration_strategy: Deploy subtree authz in log-only mode behind contract_authz flag (record would-be rejects).
Review logs for legitimate edits misclassified; refine subtree detection.
Enforce for viewer role first; verify body edits blocked, comment edits allowed.
Ramp to all roles; wire the reject->client rollback path.
Add 30s permission cache; test role downgrade mid-session.
Remove the apply-all path.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a contract doc with 'body' and 'comments' subtrees. Viewer test: a viewer submits an update touching 'body'; assert rejected with reason and doc unchanged; a viewer update touching only 'comments' is accepted. Editor test: editor update to body accepted. Mixed test: an update touching both body and comments from a viewer is fully rejected (no partial apply). Side-effect: rejected update not broadcast. Permission-change test: downgrade a user to viewer; within 30s their body edits start rejecting (cache TTL). Chaos test: craft an update that changes a deep node under body; assert the deep change is detected and blocked.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the subtree boundary, applyUpdate integrates whatever structs the client sends regardless of which part of the doc they target, so a viewer whose UI merely hid the body editor can hand-craft an update mutating the legal body and the server commits it — making role permissions purely cosmetic.

title: Stop image/blob bloat in Yjs docs by storing media out-of-band and referencing by id instead of embedding base64
business_context: A collaborative moodboard tool embeds pasted images as base64 directly into the Yjs doc. A board with 40 images grows the Y.Doc to 80MB, so every new collaborator downloads 80MB on join (slow, costly) and the WS server buffers huge updates, OOMing on image-heavy boards. Designers paste screenshots constantly. 2k boards, some with 100+ images.
constraints: Y.Doc must stay small (KB) regardless of image count
Images uploaded once, referenced by id; dedupe identical pastes
New collaborator join must not download image bytes through the Y.Doc
Images load lazily as they enter the viewport
Existing boards with embedded base64 must migrate

before_state.description: Pasted images stored as base64 strings inside Y.Map entries; doc balloons; joins transfer all image bytes.

files.content:
import * as Y from 'yjs';
export function pasteImage(ydoc:Y.Doc, dataUrl:string, x:number, y:number){ ydoc.getMap('items').set(crypto.randomUUID(), { type:'image', data: dataUrl, x, y }); /* base64 in doc */ }
issues: Base64 image bytes live in the CRDT -> 80MB docs
Every join syncs all image bytes
No dedupe; same paste stored repeatedly

reasoning: Naive fix: gzip the doc. Marginal — base64 of compressed images barely shrinks and the doc still carries all bytes. Rejected storing images in a sibling Yjs subdoc — still syncs bytes to all peers. Chosen: upload image bytes to object storage (S3/R2) keyed by a content hash (dedupe), store only the small reference {assetId, w, h, x, y} in the Y.Doc, and lazy-load image bytes via signed URLs when items enter the viewport. Cross-layer trace: paste handler hashes bytes, uploads if new (content-addressed dedupe), writes a tiny reference into the Y.Doc; renderer fetches the blob lazily via a signed URL on viewport intersection; join transfers only references; legacy base64 migrates by extracting and uploading; tests assert doc size bound and dedupe.

after_state.description: Content-addressed image upload to object storage with dedupe; Y.Doc holds tiny references; lazy viewport loading via signed URLs; legacy base64 migrated out.

files.purpose: Uploads image bytes content-addressed (auto-deduped) and stores only a small reference in the Y.Doc, keeping it KB-sized.
Loads image bytes only when the item scrolls into view, via a signed URL, so joins and scrolling stay fast.
Extracts embedded base64 images, uploads them content-addressed, and rewrites doc entries to references, shrinking legacy boards.

migration_strategy: Add asset upload + reference paste behind media_offload flag; legacy base64 paste stays.
Run extractBase64 lazily on first open of legacy boards; verify rendering parity.
Canary image-heavy boards; measure doc size and join time drop.
Ramp to all; auto-skip migration for assets that fail upload (keep base64 as fallback).
Enable lazy loading and dedupe everywhere.
Drop base64 support after all boards migrated.
testing_strategy: Fixture: a board Y.Doc. Size test: paste 40 images; assert Y.Doc encoded size stays in KB (only references). Dedupe test: paste the same image twice; assert one upload (same content hash) and two references. Join test: a new peer syncs the doc; assert no image bytes transferred via Yjs (only refs). Lazy test: items outside viewport don't fetch; entering viewport triggers one signed-URL fetch. Migration test: a legacy base64 board migrates to references with identical rendering. Chaos test: asset upload fails; assert paste falls back gracefully and doc isn't corrupted.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the join boundary, embedding base64 means the image bytes are part of the CRDT state, so the Yjs sync step2 sends all 80MB to every new collaborator and the WS server must buffer multi-megabyte update frames, OOMing on boards with many large pastes.
